speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Laura Brackett Albright took place at her home on Potlatch River below Juliaetta on June 23rd, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I don't know how it is that you knew that you were a Republican and got involved in politics to start with. Okay. The reason that I'm a Republican is because my family, it's a family tradition and a family philosophy, because in the old days it seems that the Republican Party was the party who faced up to money issues. Now, I inherited it and when I was old enough to, and of course in those days you had to be 21, you couldn't do it at 18, you know, you had to be 21 before you could register. So I was married. I was married when I was 20 and before I could vote, you see, I had been married and I had to face up to some things. I think I voted as a family tradition at first, but when my children were born and we lost our first baby, a little boy that was tragic as far as I was concerned, then our youngsters came along quickly and in about six years I was faced with getting my children into school. And when you're on a homestead and a farm and across the river and the railroad and a few other things and the schools, one at Arrow and one at Pilot Rock, Pilot Rock's where I taught, you see, and I thought that's our district that I would send him there. You can't send a six -year -old youngster walking these miles. You've got to do something about getting to school. And so I convinced to think about the school system. And Sam, it was either break up my home and go to Lewiston or get decent schools out here. And when I say decent schools, I was looking to the future because the one -room schools, I didn't have anything against them. If we got good teachers, the only thing that ever limited a district school, a one -room district school was the caliber of your citizens who were on the school board and your teacher. And usually it was the quality of the teacher that you hired. And in those days we got all of $60 a month. And there weren't many, naturally, the other teachers. I was the one that was raising my certificate, the grade of my certificate. And, but anyway, so in order to have consolidation of schools, you've got to have roads. And in order to get roads, Sam, you have to have taxes. And when you get taxes. So I made it my business to find out all I could about school and school taxes. And the person that I went to, this may be interesting to you, I spent my second year in high school in Lewiston working for my board. And my superintendent was Joel Jennifer, an old, wonderful, old superintendent teacher, Joel Jennifer. And he was elected to the legislature about that time because he too was yelling about taxes for school, for education. And he sat down several times with me in Lewiston and he said, now your open door is consolidation. And he said, in order to do this, and then he laid it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so I said, okay, what do I get to learn about taxes?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And I know who pays them, but I don't know the extent because that was about the time the income tax came in, you see. And so I studied on that. Then I took to the road. And as it happened, I was appointed to the board when the state of Idaho decided that they needed to consolidate schools. And I consolidated all those schools.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did this happen?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: It started in 1947, through 51 and 52.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you feel prior to that that consolidation was a necessity? What made you decide you had to consolidate?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I had to consolidate to get schools in this area or break up my family and go to Lewiston. And it was, I couldn't take my husband there because of his health. He had a health problem and he was the very first mechanic in Moscow, Idaho. If you talk up there, you'll find that he worked when he was going to school for his uncle who had a bicycle shop up there. About that time, why the automobile came and Dr. O, Dr. Gritman was too old to fight the weather and to do the driving and so Raleigh used to drive for him. And he fell on a railroad gate out here and dislocated two vertebrae in his back that affected his kidneys. And we've had this illness to contend with all our lives. And Raleigh could not go to town and be a mechanic and that was when he was trained. So we proved up on this homestead over here, you see, and went to market gardening because Raleigh turned out to be a pretty good salesman. And that's another story anyway. When my folks found out that I was going to marry Raleigh. They were too excited about it at first. A vegetable peddler.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But your kids, weren't they through school by the end of Second World War, by the time consolidation finally came around?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yes, but consolidation had been going on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Prior to that?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yes, I mean, prior to that, I thought that Juliaetta would be the place to consolidate. And we ran into a lot of opposition on the ridges because those kids would have to come into Juliaetta. And it was never thought of to provide buses in those days. You see, this was a later thing. And then Lapway commenced to consolidate and cul -de -sac commenced to bring in some extra things. And when I finally was appointed to the legislature, I was appointed in 1949 and that was two years after the 1947 when the legislature finally decided that consolidation should go into the state law. And, oh, you know, I'd been on the school board for 41 years and I should know something about the laws and all this sort of thing. And having a gift of gab is just...

speaker: Lora Albright
words: You make you a natural. So my husband says, well, he said, honey, because I had to go be gone. I mean, I was out of the home a good deal. And in the summertime, of course, we always had housekeepers because after all, the family had to be the field people. And poor Ollie was left lots of times because there was night work and what have you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he supportive of the idea of being involved in these activities?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yes. That's what he said. He says, well, honey, you got the gift of gab and you want to do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, why don't you go do it?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: He says, I'll stay home and keep the home fires burning. Bless his heart. People wonder why I go down and take care of him as well as I do. He's in the nursing home. And so we got consolidation. Let me ask you, when you talk about consolidation, one of the things that I had heard from somebody that came from this area was that Juliaetta was reluctant to consolidate down here, partly because they didn't want a lot of Indian kids going to school with them. And that, in fact, in doing so, they lost a lot of the tax advantages that this area had going for it because of the railroad taxes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, that's true. It's true. I learned about Indians when I came down here to teach. Clarence Mox Mox was in the second grade and he was 13 years old. And at that time they didn't realize that he was retarded. They thought he was just a slothful little Indian kid and he wasn't so little. Big fat youngster. And when I came in to teach there, I had two other Indian youngsters that were just as sharp as they could be. Nice kids. And I had two other white youngsters that were retarded. And that's when I realized that Clarence was protecting himself because they do this. Another part of my history is that my youngest brother was born retarded. And because I had lived with Eddie, you know, of course he was just a youngster when I was married. In fact, he died the year that I was married. But they protect themselves by keeping you away. They want to be just like everybody else and when they realize that it's hard for them to read, to take it in, sometimes to even hear, or to assimilate and to use what they do here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do they do? How do they protect themselves?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well, two ways. One is they act the clown. And the other one was they saw and make you believe that they know all about it, but can't be bothered. Not soak exactly. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the word you used? Soap?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I don't know it. And so, anyway, that was a euphemism. Uh huh. So, I had, in self -protection, Clarence would make believe that he had his lessons and he knew all about it and he couldn't read with a hoot, you know. And of course I knew it as a teacher and I was supposed to teach that kid something. And I worked like everything to teach him and I don't think I ever taught him a thing except I did teach him some manners. But he wanted to learn. He wanted to learn. And so it was difficult, but if I hadn't had this background on retired edition, I never would have realized what Clarence was doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say he taught him manners, what kind of manners?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: When he came in, he would wipe his feet before coming into the schoolhouse. And he would put his lunch bucket, which sometimes he didn't have a thing in that, but terrible things. But he would put his lunch bucket under the hook where he hung his clothes in the coat room. And when he came in, he would say, good morning, our youngsters. And you see, when I played the organ, I had to turn the organ around to watch these youngsters or play with my back to my youngsters. Well, that isn't good either, you know. And so he used to sit there this way and I, so I caught him after, yeah, caught him after school. He couldn't read the, see that was early. I didn't realize that he couldn't read the words to America, for instance, you know, and learn them by rote and so on. And I realized he couldn't because he was only in the second grade. And a youngster that size, only in the second grade there's something wrong. And then I realized that in just two days that he was retired, that, and so then I had to start working on the other angle. Uh huh. Well, it seemed like... And he did, he responded to me when he realized that I wasn't forcing him to do something that he couldn't do. And I wasn't maligning him. Now this is a good $64 word to say, well, if you don't, you know, that, but he was doing it. And when he did do something, I praised him. I said, well, good Clarence, that's the first time you've done this or something like this. And of course the other kids would sometimes would titter and I'd have to spank them. And then I had two white youngsters there that were retired that came from West Virginia background. Uh, and I had to be very careful because they were a little bit, I mean, they wouldn't be put in the same class with Indians. Oh my goodness sakes. Yeah, I know. So we couldn't consolidate with Juliaetta. So I, they were in Lato County anyhow, so I came over and worked in Esper's County.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When your children went to school, was there any consolidation when your own children were in school?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were they, where did they go?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well, we sent them to Lewis and Sam because Lapway was in the middle of a terrible fight that started in the Grange. And it held over to the school. And our youngsters, when consolidation finally came, you see sometimes our youngsters went to Arrow and sometimes to Pilot Rock. Well, at Arrow and there in Nespur has been Pilot Rock, the others. Oh my God. And so, yeah. And so they, I didn't want them to go to Lapway and be involved in that. And so we had to buy a car for them to go anyway. So we sent them to Lewiston and our youngsters all graduated from Lewiston.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that from the high school?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yes, from high school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did they switch to Lewiston when they started in high school pretty much?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yes, because in the eighth grade they were in the rural school, so eighth grade because I was on the school board.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could they have gone to Kendra, Juliaetta if they'd have wanted to?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: No, no. I mean we would have, yeah, we probably could have sent them if we didn't want to. But as long as we had to get the car, we decided that we would send them. And James, my son James, about that time, was a Husky youngster and they wanted him to play football in Lewiston naturally. And we didn't want him to play football because there were so many are injured for life in football. And so he had a music sense, our youngsters, all except our oldest daughter, been very musical. And so we suggested that instead of doing the football thing that he join the orchestra. Well, he tried singing, but he was a big youngster at 14. His voice was changing and he didn't take the singing. So he got a violin, not a violin, not a mandolin, the big one, the big bowl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is it?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Slapped a bass. Yeah, slapped the bass. And he slapped the bass and after he got out of high school, he even joined the union and did it commercially. That was his avocation. And so that turned out real nice. I think it was nice for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the conflict at Lapway?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I don't know. It was something I just heard about it and people were it. But it was something that started in the Grange and then came into the school system. And I just said to Raleigh, as long as we have to buy a car. So we did. We bought it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how to divide along the lines of Indians and whites?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I don't know what it was. I really don't know. It was something of when they were building that school house, the new school house there. And I don't know what it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when they really consolidated, they came into, are you familiar with it?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: They had to appoint a local committee, you know, for consolidation. And they took over all the assets of the school and whatever they decided to do with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And oh, why?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: They sold stuff that the school could use.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why would they sell it?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And I don't want to go into it because some of them said it was because they wanted to buy cheap and the people that were on the committee made it possible for them to do it. And this...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Buy what? The supplies?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Oh, like there was trucks, there were chairs, there were desks, there were little stuff like this. There was a... I think there was some equipment that they used, maintenance equipment. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were sold away from the district?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they lost things that they wouldn't have for them? So they just consolidated the school?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Like Longmore and things like this. Well, they just went beyond their, I mean, beyond the intent of the law. Well, so I decided to shut them. Well, it wouldn't be a very good school without a lot of that stuff. Well, I know. I know. And I didn't want to be involved in it and I didn't want my children involved in it. The Lapley School has come a long way. Then way down the line after my kids were all out, I'd been on that school for so long. Then Governor Jordan, who knew some of the things, appointed me on that. And Cuddlesack had just built their new school and they were so afraid that they were going to lose that. And I said, well, so they sent me to talk to them and I said, well, no. I said, goodness sakes. You can keep your own high school if you can meet the requirements. But I said, you'd be silly to send your kids up to the eighth grade, up to junior high school down there. And I said, keep your, Longmore was the same way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Keep your what?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Keep your school here. Keep your elementary school right there. And keep your schoolhouse and the equipment for your town center, your city center. That was another thing. When you lose your school, you lose your city center.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, they had it through high school, didn't they?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: They had it through high school, but now you see those kids all come down to Lapley.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They do?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, that is, a lot of them do. Because it is a larger and it's a better high school than, go on and say. But anyhow, this was one of the things. Longmore had a new school. They thought we were going to, and you know, Sam, it was an amazing thing after all of this and I made it possible for them to keep them. I say I, I'm talking because I worked with the committee and we made it possible for them to keep them. Then a lot of the families turned around and said, well, for instance in the Gifford area, we want to send our little kids where our big kids go. So then it started busing. And then my homework on the tax situation came in real handy. Because you can't have buses without roads and you can't get roads without taxes and you can't get taxes without people voting it. So there. It's just as simple as that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The way it happened was that they tended to consolidate the high school level first?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: No, yes. Yes they did. Because the little youngsters, some of those little youngsters would have had to ride an hour on a bus in those days because the roads were bad. And because the high school centers were far away. And it wasn't too bad. Some of the kids, for instance, I rode a horse back three miles, six miles a day, thought nothing of it. I mean, it was very happy. I had a good horse that I could ride. But it's, you can see how they had to do. Because when the big kids, who would be able to stand the storms and things, get on a bus to ride 20 miles, and then the little kids have to walk four or five miles or else their mothers and fathers take them. You see what I'm talking about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But for the parents to want their little kids to go far away to school by bus, wasn't that pretty much the same thing as a dagger to the community? I mean, weren't they losing their local schools?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: They did, Sam. But about this time, people commenced to get cars. You see, you can't imagine a time when everybody didn't have a car. But I was 18 years old before I ever had my first car ride. You see, this is the difference in our ages and in the times. And as people got cars, it wasn't a big hardship to go to Lapway or to even go to Lewiston or to go where they needed to.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And this had a great bearing on it, because then they now have their schools in Gifford, and the people are using it, like our Spalding Schoolhouse, the women's groups, the men's groups, the Granges, the places to vote, and all of this. And gatherings are because they still had their elementary. And the elementary youngsters, it seemed like a long time, but you see, they could get on that bus and get to school warm, safe, not like having to walk. Some of the youngsters that came to school to me had to walk three miles, going and coming, and through storm and what have you. Give me a little idea of how you went about raising the tax monies to improve the roads to make it possible for the schools to be consolidated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it you had to do?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I don't understand the legislation that made this possible or anything. Well, the legislation, I didn't have so much to do with that. I did vote on some of the others. But the first thing, you had to convince the parents that that's what they wanted for their children. And I made it my business, Sam, to have every possible approach of what those kids could get in school when a larger group came together and had a larger community from which to choose PTA. PTA. I talk PTA. And then they got, through the years, they got fed up with PTA and they are now PTOs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what the difference is, don't you?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well, the PTA is the national organization that you belong to. The PTO is just a little organization of local people, organization. And it's alright. I mean, I don't yell about it because my children are out. I have grandchildren. Now, Sam, I'm real happy. I have six great grandchildren. Step as it happens to be, going right to school from our old road out here. Too happy. So you convinced people to do that. So I commenced to talk about the possible programs. And one of them was home economics.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I'll never forget, the grandfather said, home economics?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well, our girls are learning to cook and to sew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do they need home economics for? So then I would say, oh, well, did you know about the problems of canning?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Now, I says, in home economics, they will learn the newest thing. You get a teacher that has earned her degree or his. It can be either a man or a woman. And then I would talk to them. I'd say, well, your boys can get carpentry. They can get farm skills. And I said, and think about, talk to the farmers. In the judging, of course, part of this was 4 -H. And I would talk about 4 -H because this depends entirely on local leadership.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, who in your community now is well enough versed to teach your kids?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I says, I understand that you're teaching them everything you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But wouldn't it be, wouldn't you like them to know something else?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And about that time, the automobile was coming in. And I said, and I would say, frankly, my youngsters are going to Lewiston. And I said, we did this because of the organization and the programs in the school. And there's no reason why you can't have it. Because in the state of Idaho, education has a high priority if you're willing to pay for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, now, why don't you try among your to see if you can't get some volunteers to take your youngsters to school?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: You know, just try it and see what it entails.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean to drive them into another school?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: For families. And some of them did. And they commenced to realize.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then one of the men says, well, are you sharing yours?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I said, yes, there are six girls that are riding to Lewiston to the normal school with our kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they said, well, how much are they paying?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I said, all we asked them to do is to get their own insurance. Because I said, the car goes anyway. And we have to furnish the gas to get our own youngsters there. And those youngsters, mine's all right. And they can't go if they don't. And there's about 26 people in this community that got degrees.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Lewiston from normal college?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Especially if your youngsters are in debate. And I tried that. That was another thing I did. I said, now there's music, there's debate, there is horseshoeing, there is animal husbandry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you want on your local?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And then I would take the new brochure of the state. And some of those old fellows never looked at that. They didn't know that that was available. They could hire teachers that had these skills. Reading and writing and arithmetic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where do you think that they're, or why do you think they were resistant, or where the resistance came to consolidation? I mean, what was the real basis?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: The real basis of that were the grandpas and the grandmas that had already paid to have their youngsters. And they very often owned the land, you see, the farms. And the young people were farming it, it's true. And they had to be convinced. And sometimes the larger farms were then being formed. I remember down in the Lewiston orchards between that old Lindsay Creek road, those big farms were out there. Well, those people couldn't have cared less. They couldn't have cared less. And they had to be outvoted. And I said, you get in there and do your voting. If you're interested in your children, you get in there and do your voting. Because these big people that own all these big farms are not going to vote your children's education.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What else did I say?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: It sounds very persuasive to me. Well, listen, then, now let's say that there was agreement more or less in the community, or most of the people came to favor consolidation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then what was the next step to get it? What did they have to do to actually get consolidated once they believed in it?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: They tried the trial and error, but there was really no overall consolidation until it was passed by the legislature. Until the legislature voted. And as I remember, I think that bill passed in 1947. I think it was introduced first in 43, 45. You see, it's a two year odd thing. And I didn't pay too much attention to it because the Albrights had solved theirs. I mean, we had to buy the car and send our kids to Austin. On a trial and error basis. Anyway, I have to turn it off. I have had the idea that it did have effect on the small communities. And I'm wondering what you think about that because it did, it seems to me, end or really strike a blow at the real little communities which had been centered around these small tiny schools that then went out of business. Sam, this is true. And yet, about this time, the thing that was set up that helped it was the voting, the places to vote. And at first, we thought that we, for instance, would be going into Lapway to vote. But in those days, they didn't have the electronic, and you had to have two sets of elections, those that were voting the people and those that were counting. And if you had more than 675 votes, that's all you could count in one day and do it. And in order to get people to work on the election boards, they had to have different ones. And so, a lot of these small school houses were the voting precincts. You know, at Leland right now, you just go around, Gifford, it's at the school house. And so, it just fell into place because cars came in and these people could get there. And there, it was a different ballgame entirely. A different era. Uh huh. The way you describe to me the role of the teacher in the community, in the small community, now it seems like that would have changed if the teacher wasn't there anymore. I mean, she was in a larger community. No, no, no, that's not true because the larger community is true, but like, I'd like to go back to the Lapway, cul -de -sac, Gifford, Lenoir areas. They had their, what they call their elementary attendance. Lenoir still has the school up there, you know. And so does Gifford and so do all of these. And so, they simply used the public because they, people won't go 10 miles to vote. They won't go, they just won't go. You've got to have. And now, of course, with the electronic stuff that we have, they could have larger precincts, but because they've always used it. Now, even in Spalding, there's a line of voters there. And we have a large precinct, it's true. And some of the precincts are larger, but it's still in the small communities. You'll find that this followed. And so, I don't think there was much of a rupture there. I can't remember ever thinking of it. Your kids, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And now, we have local grocery stores, but how many people will go to Lewiston to buy this, that, and the other?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And they used to have to buy your overalls right across the counter. One of those things, and your groceries. But it's a different era, naturally.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Now, our youngsters are even flying. I won't say that because there's no use putting it on there, but I've got a grandson that's just home from Phoenix. He flew home. He's taking it and will fly back this next week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When it was on a voluntary basis, before the legislature passed the law, what kind of consolidation was possible if people believed it?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Through voting, if they wanted to. They could vote to join or vote to enlarge or whatever. But, on the other hand, they would consider how to get their kids so much further. You see, in the first place, why, you either rode a horse or you walked to get school. Well, we went through that. And then, as people settled in, we're going through sort of a thing like this now. This canyon is being divided up into small five -acre lots, wherever they can, because people want to retire from the prairies where the winters are cold and what have you. Yet, they would like to have their chickens, their pig, and their cow. This is a new era. But, if you want to get somewhere in a hurry, like my kids who come home from Phoenix, their people have to take it. You're talking about progress, really. I mean, about things changing. Yeah, it was progress. And I'm wondering, looking back on it then, this is something I've been thinking about a lot lately. Looking back on it in the early days, how people felt about progress. You said to me, for instance, Mrs. Weibark was really strong on getting a better life, having things improve.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that was a general feeling that most people had, that they were real receptive to the idea of progress? Or was there real divided opinion?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Not a division, but everybody didn't see eye -to -eye, Sam. It's just like any new thing that comes in. Some, as I say, the grandpas and grandmas that owned the farms said, well, we got along. We got along all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And why do the kids need art and dramatics and all of this sort of thing, even music?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Now, some of them were very happy to have a teacher that could teach English and music, you know, this sort of thing, and get them to schools. And most of the people were anxious for their, and I use the word anxious rather than eager, because they, when it's your children's and your grandchildren's future, sometimes you're anxious as well as eager. And this, most people wanted to do the best they can for their children. And most people looked ahead and realized we didn't have telephones a lot. We, for years and years, the Albright's were the only ones had a telephone around here. And, gee, illness or accidents or what have you, you were the center, you know, they'd go to gravitate to the nearest one. And so, everything brought its own step forward. I don't know how else to say it. And as you assimilate each new step forward in progress, then you're open for what comes afterwards. I don't know how else to say it. That's the only way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did people feel, how did they feel, education for children is one thing, but how about their attitude towards more modern homes and telephone service and electricity and all that? Was there, was it more eager for that?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yes, yeah, they were very, the telephone went through early. If you talk to the Shufers, you'll... Yes, I have. Yes, you have. And we thought that we could get a telephone from Lewis & Oud. We'd have to pay, build, pay for having the line built and then give it to the company. And this was hard for us because it was about $1200 a mile or something like this. And in those days, it was difficult for us with our children and so on. But they wanted those, they wanted those good things, just like the farmers wanted this new machinery. They didn't want to have to have horses all the time, you know, as soon as they could mechanize. And so, I think as soon as they, yeah, they wanted it as soon as they could. As soon as they could get it. And one of the things that they were slow about was medical services. We lost a lot of hours to illness that might have been prevented if we had had an overall medical service. And we still don't have that today. We just don't have that. You know how scarce doctors are now. Sometimes I wonder what's going to happen to people. We have insurance and we have all of this sort of things, but it's the opening of a new era. And some of these days, I'm supposing that there will be an overall medical program. I'm just as sure as there is now an overall educational program because everyone will benefit from that. And I'm looking forward to it. I know that I'll live long enough, but I'm looking forward to it. That's the next thing that I think we need to work on. You know, I was thinking about when you were first married and whether you looked at the homestead living that you did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you look back on it, was it, we talked about some, was it more of a hardship than a pleasure? Or did you find it a great deal of fun as well? Or at the time, was it just hard, plain hard work?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: It was hard work. It was hard work, of course, but there was satisfaction of accomplishment there, Sam. We, Raleigh and I didn't have the best of the land because a lot of the homesteads, you see, had been taken before that. And so, but we did take what we thought would enter into our own family use and took the 160 acres across the river that spans both sides. And then proved up on that because at the time, my father -in -law had, was in the sheep business and this would have been perfect sheep business. And then just having the land gives you a feeling of permanence. Nobody could come along and kick you off your home because after all you did own it. And the old homestead shack, yes, was difficult. And we had to, I had to carry water halfway up that hill for washing. Raleigh helped me. We had to bring our drinking water from the folks over there and along the hillside. I killed my first rattlesnakes and I was scared to death of them over there. And I was all alone when I did it. You've heard that story. No, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is that story? Well, we had, I'll have to take you some time and show you the, you know where the Johnson house is?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yes. Well, that's where the, alright, that was the alright place, you see. And we had to go along that hillside. Our homestead was in one of these drawers up here and that hill is ours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Across the river?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah. And so I was coming home, Raleigh was working with the folks in this market gardening business because that was our bread and butter. We were living on the homestead in a 14 by 16 foot shack. And it was dug into the corner back here, it was four feet in the bank and there was eight feet pole up on the end of that one to keep the porch up. And I had to go down, oh, about 20 feet and then go along that hillside. And there was a bunch of rock right by that hillside. So I was coming along here, hurrying, time to get supper before Raleigh came home. And I had this bottle of water, a quart bottle of drinking water. And it was a pill and it was hot and I was sweating. And when I got there, I thought, that's good. The old ram didn't put me up a stump and the rattlesnakes hadn't done anything about me. And I turned around and looked across the valley and I thought, oh, I made it one. Zip, zip right down here. And there was that rattlesnake between me and my porch. And the first thing I did was to throw the bottle of water and I broke the bottle of water. And all he did was just duck under a rock and keep right on a zip. And I thought, I've got to kill that snake. And I'd heard that they jumped at you and all this sort of thing, which isn't true, by the way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I thought, well, what can I do?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And he got in the rocks and there he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what could you do?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: So I went by the porch and there was a 14 foot pole that kept my clothesline up. So I took that pole and I went there and I gingerly took the rocks off until I got down to the poor little snake. He was a little one. He was only two feet long and he only had 10 rattles. But I got him out there and I belabored him and I killed him. And I really killed him because I had to prove to my husband that I killed my first rattlesnake on my own.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And after that, after you killed him, I then... It doesn't seem so fearful anymore. You learn to live with it just like you learn to live with anything that you have to. Around here, there's rattlesnakes all over here. And I've been looking at the grass out here with the youngsters, but we teach them. I made it my business to find out about it. And I... the best... I told you this. The best kit was the forestry kit. And so I went to the forestry people in Lewiston and I said, I need a rattlesnake kit and I want to know how to do it, how to use it. This trinacut business, you know, I didn't know about the trinacut and the slicing into the bite and they even suggested that you suck it. Well, I couldn't possibly have done it. I just couldn't have done it unless it had been maybe one of my children or something. And so the forestry has a suction cup because lots of times those fellows might have been bitten where they couldn't get to it themselves. And so I got the kit and I learned to use it. I never had to use it, thank God, but I learned to use it because I had to for the safety of my children and for my own peace of mind. Do you know whether people here believe that a snake would...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: if one snake was killed, that its mate would come to that place?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: That's something that I've heard that you haven't watched for another rattlesnake. I don't know, maybe it's just an old video. Many times they go in pairs where you find one. That's what they told me. Where you find one, you're liable to find two. Well, only in my lifetime have I found, well, two times where there was two snakes together. I wouldn't be quoted as an expert at all because rattlesnakes around here is where you find them. Big ones, little ones, indifferent ones. The only thing that I have stressed on our children, a little one can poison you just as much as a big one. And you don't want to take any chances. And now with our telephones and with the quick... and with the vaccine, this is another thing, with the serum, I should say, with the serum, not vaccine because that's prevented, but the serum. It isn't as dangerous. It's dangerous, don't mistake me. I'm not talking it down. And you don't go out and invite a rattlesnake bite. You just watch your p's and q's. But if you were bitten with the quick telephone, with the car service, and with the hospitals as close as they are, and I think, I know that the Lewiston hospitals do have the serum in rattlesnake country at the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were kids in the woods, how did they take the precautions? Did they look before stepping on the rocks and that kind of thing?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: No, well, I don't think so. Up in our high woods, you don't have the rattlesnakes. You see, I grew up in the area, Rubens area, and so it's just around here. And we don't. We keep the hose and the shovels handy because that's what we kill them with. And we do kill them. It really is the only thing that we kill, really. Because I've never found any way at all, Sam, where rattlesnakes and people can just live in the same place at the same time without some risk. We don't kill them on the hillsides. We don't kill a rattlesnake on the hillsides when it's out in the pasture because they're wonderful policemen. The mice and the gophers that eat up the range, this is the main food for a rattlesnake. They'll go down in the holes and the rattlesnake go down in those. Of course, they eat a few bunnies too, but then it's one of those things. So you don't kill a rattlesnake if you're smart out on the hillsides. When we were haying, we sometimes would run into them and get them on the load by mistake, but you just take the pitchfork and throw them off. And then we would kill those because, well, it was just natural to when you get them on the load and you kill them. That's one of the things. That's interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There's one thing about the homestead that I want to ask you, which may seem like a silly question, but was there romance to it to you as well as hard work? Or would romance be just what a modern person has never experienced?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: There was a satisfaction, Sam, I think. And then Raleigh particularly used to say, well, we've done such and such. We'd set our mark for $10 ,000. We were going to make $10 ,000 a share because we wanted to buy a home that was on the road. There's no road over there. We had to cross the river in a boat. And when the river's high, you couldn't cross the river. You didn't get any mail or anything. And if you had a doctor or if you'd had an emergency, you're stuck there. That's all there was to it. That's when I learned about old pioneering medicine and this sort of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a boat did you use?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Oh, just a little.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A rowboat?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yes, just a rowboat because that's all there was. That's all we had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you stay over there for days at a time without coming over?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Oh, yeah. Oh, yes. Yes, sure. And in the wintertime, why, Raleigh sometimes would go over and get the mail and I just wouldn't be out of there for a long time. What we did for entertainment might be interesting for you. I, having been a teacher, we used to... One reason that I'm sentimental about the Saturday Evening Post, we got a Saturday Evening Post every week. And Raleigh would be tired. And so he would lay on the bed. See, we had the bed and the little cookstove and everything we had and the old chair in a 12 by 16. So I would sit there and read and Raleigh would... Oh, maybe I should tell you this. In order to prove up on her homestead, we had to plow up any place that we could plow. So the top of that hill had a place of about three acres that we could plow. So Raleigh borrowed the horses from his people and we took the horses up there and plowed that and planted it to beans. Because, you know, the pioneers lived and grew up on beans and Boston baked beans was the heritage that I brought to the family. Because Ma Albright just had beans that she would bake or boil like bean soup with a ham hock. She didn't know about the Boston baked beans, but I did. And so when I came into the family, why, I would bake Boston baked beans. And this was a very popular thing. And so we planted beans up there. Well, when they were ready to harvest, Raleigh went up and took some gunny sacks and flayed them out with a pitchfork on a canvas. And then he put those beans in half gunny sacks because we had to get them down off that mountain. And he took the old mare and built a stone boat. Now a stone boat is a long wooden thing with a single tree and the horse doesn't have any way to hold it back because just the tugs. And so you teach her to go on the sideways and then you turn around this way and go a little bit lower and lower. And Raleigh had the gunny sacks of these beans. And as I remember, there were eight half gunny sacks of those beans that he'd flailed out up there, which is our winner. And so he tied them with some wire. I think it was clothesline wire on this boat, stone boat. And then he took old fly up there and hitched her under the stone boat to come down with the beans. Well, when he got halfway down, there is a rock slide, what we call, which is like the rocks. You see them. And the trail went across that. Well, the horse walked in the trail, but the stone boat was too wide. And when that hit it, it slid over and turned upside down and spilled the beans and they rolled clear down that hill and spilled beans all over creation and what have you. Well, Raleigh was, felt pretty badly about it and it was dark. And so he came home and that night it rained.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the next day, why, what do we do?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And he said, well, we've got to go get him. He said, after all of this work, he said, come and you lead the horse and I'll get the beans. So we went up there and picked up as many beans. Part of the sacks had been torn and some of them were half empty and all this sort of thing. But we picked up the beans that we had and I suppose we had, oh, 125 pounds maybe of beans and we brought them down to the shack.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do you do with them? Where in the world could we dry those beans?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And we talked of putting them under the house because you see there was a big place under the house. But this was the, about the 15th of October. My husband's birthday is the 14th of October and I remember it was his birthday when I got those beans. And when I looked at them, when we looked at them, got them down, they were wet even to being wrinkly, Sam. You know, that's just real damp for beans. So the only place that there was, they had to be in the house and I said, well, honey, I don't know what in the world you would do with them because here was this little cook stove. It was the only heat we had and Raleigh had it on a wooden base, you know, a little because I was a tall girl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And we had, and so how could we?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And I had a little old steamer trunk that we used for Davenport. So, oh, the only place was under the bed. So Raleigh brought some six inch boards and put around under the bed and we poured our beans under there so that we could stir them every day. And we dried those beans under the bed. Well, after we got them dried, Sam, there were some of them that were moldy. And so Raleigh said, well, since I did it, and so he sat there evening after evening after evening, sorting those beans on the table. The only light we had was, and I sat over here and read the Saturday Evening Post. Read it out loud to him. And he would read out loud. And he, as Raleigh would say, well, honey, after all, he said, I don't read as well as you do. You read. Read me the story and I'll sort the beans. And so we spent the winter, I mean, entertainment, you see. And so I've always loved Saturday Evening Post and that's really all we could afford, Sam. And every week we got a Saturday Evening Post. And now it only comes, you know, once in a while. You don't have it every week like you used to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had those beans, were most of those beans still in the sacks? Or did many?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, the ones that were scattered up and down the hill, I told Raleigh, I said, don't bother. I said, if you can get at least even a half a sack, whatever you can get. And we took the horse up there and with the saddle, I had my saddle in those days. And we brought them down on the saddle, you see. We didn't try to take the... You had them individually.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you able to sell any of them?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: No, no, no, no. We just had them to eat. No, we couldn't, no. But I think of entertainment, all those years that we were over there. We had three years over there. Raleigh had filed on the homestead earlier. It took five years. But we had to cultivate that little field up there every year and finally we put it to Alfalfa. We put it to wheat one year because it had to be something that contributed to our welfare, you see. And so we had cows and we brought, that time, we took it over the other way on the other side, which is, there was a way that we could get down a little bit earlier, a little bit easier. And we brought, we put it to wheat, brashed out the wheat and brought it down and then we planted it to Alfalfa and used it for pasture and the cattle ate it off. So that's that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Saturday Evening Post was really the one entertainment thing?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, the Saturday Evening...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the one entertainment thing?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, that we, yes it was when we'd go over because, oh, we had parties. We had over to Marlbrite's and the family parties and so on and our school was still, we still had our school up there. You know, one thing about this, I was asking you a little bit before about opportunities for women. It occurred to me that it seems very rarely could women continue to teach after they were married. I know that most districts wouldn't let them. And I'm wondering why that was. No, I don't think so. I don't think it was that, Sam, as much as it was that when a woman got married, she was so busy with her life and her family that it usually was old maids or widows that taught. And if they were good teachers, I don't think they had so much trouble, Sam. I really can't remember. I'm wondering if they wanted to. Yeah, mhm. Yes, if they, and if they were good teachers, I don't think so because some of the best teachers that we had were Mrs. Porter down here, for instance. Her husband was drowned here in the Clarewater, you know. And she was one of the best teachers we had and she taught both at Arrow and at our Pilot Rock. And we enjoyed the older women teachers for two reasons. One was that if they were still teaching at that time, Sam, they liked to teach. I liked to teach. It's wonderful to teach. When my daughter -in -law was teaching at Lapway, I was her substitute. And I didn't think that they'd let me substitute because mine was just an old -fashioned elementary teacher. And this was high school stuff. But Mr. Wilson said, oh, yes, my Mrs. Albright, fine, that'll be fine. And I had the time of my life over there with those high school kids. It's wonderful to teach. That gleam of recognition. And I got the idea that comes into a kid's eye when he's finally caught it. Nothing will ever surpass that for a real teacher who really likes to teach.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Porter's husband, how did he drown in the river?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: They were taking some cattle across and he got caught into a lasso and was dragged down the river before they could get him out. And in those days, you see, they didn't have the big roads that's what we call the south highway over there, you know. It wasn't there at all, you see. They had to go across the Clearwater River in a boat. Right opposite Arrow down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did going across the potlatch here, did that make people pause? I mean, did you have to be very careful about that?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yes. When that river is high, you don't... I used to sit up at that river because our shack on the Indian, we rented some Indian land up there. And that's where we really made the money to buy the land around here and to finally build our house down here. Because this... there's 10 parcels of land that's in this holding here. And we had to get across that river. And you couldn't get across with a car at all. You'd have to take the horses and the angle down. And we built sort of a ford because a horse too, when the current is high, cannot negotiate huge rocks. They've got to have a place and have confidence to put their feet down or else they're not going to do it. And there was only once that it came up to the edge of the porch, the back porch there. We never were flooded out. The only flood we ever had was down here when the railroad went out. But you had to live with it. You had to negotiate. And that was one reason why Raleigh usually tried to do the plowing in the fall because you couldn't get over there in time to do it in the spring. And then we would put... we never put the melons over there because that would have been impossible. But we'd put corn or potatoes or something that you harvested later, you see. So, you work with your situation. And as far as opportunity for women, the women themselves were the only limiting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As far as I know, Idaho was the very first state, Sam, did you know this, to grant women the vote? Did you know it?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I knew it was early. I didn't know it might have been the first. Yeah, it was the first state. And the reason they did it, Sam, is because they needed those votes for the population count. I think that's wonderful. I have a favorite... well, I've told you this. I have a favorite talk I used to give in the church work and what have you, that 31st chapter of Proverbs. I better turn that off.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well...

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And then it goes on to say, I don't want to do this on this tape. And your food and shelter are two things. And your food starts in pioneering or even today in a garden. And your animals and your grains, particularly. Carla Emery is doing a good job. She's got my hominy recipe in her book. And then you start with the nice things that you want to do, like extras, jam, pickles, and you can everything that you have. We couldn't get sugar in 1918. I had to can my apricots without sugar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever eat canned apricots without sugar?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And I didn't know what to do with them, so that's when we learned to drink them. And I'd put them through the sieve and drink it with ice in it, just like a cold drink, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that rough, the rationing during the first war? Was it hard on people?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, it was difficult because it was fair. It would have been much more difficult if they hadn't given each of us a ration because the rich people that could afford it would have had all of the flour and all of the sugar and all of the whatever that we had to ration. But as it was, our steps, you see, in our families, we could get our share of the sugar and the flour and then whatever we did with it. That's when you learned to make biscuits out of whole wheat, homegrown flour instead of the nice fluffy stuff that you're used to. And you used the native fruits that were naturally sweet in connection with those that were not so sweet. I mean, you learn. And now they're talking about fruit leathers. Well, we learned about that a long time ago because we dried them in the sun. All we had to do was to be able to get some mosquito bar to keep the yellow jackets from eating them up before we could get them. So you did a lot of fruit drying. Yes, they were drying. I wanted to ask you some about the vegetable, the truck gardening operation itself. Was it, now it sounded from the way you described to me, driving around, you did with Raleigh, the Albright family already had a pretty good sized truck gardening. They had to, Sam, because my father -in -law was a painter in Moscow. He painted all those original university buildings. He was one of the head painters up there. And he became poisoned, lead poisoned, because you remember in the old time paint, and Paul Bright was dying with lead poisoning and he could no longer paint up there. So they decided that they would move onto a farm where they could raise, because at that time they still had the four children, I mean they had the four boys. So Paul Bright, I don't know how he found down here, but he bought the Knowles exemption for the homestead where that White House is, where that Johnson House is now. And they moved down there. Well, then they had to make a living, you see. They had to make a living. And because of Paul's, he grew up in Moscow, he was a young man, they came from Spokane Falls down there, and Raleigh and all of the boys were born there. I guess the last boy was born here, but anyway, he decided that the market gardening, because there was little places along the river that were rich, rich soil. So they started raising these vegetables and selling them, taking them to Moscow. And it gradually grew because people loved that non -irrigated, deep, rich vegetables. And so the first thing that we always did, and Raleigh and I fell heir to it, was all those radishes and lettuce were the first ones. And then onion sets, we found out we could seed onions, but you see they didn't mature until fall. But we discovered onion sets, and then we could have the green ones. But the thing that really helped us was the melons. They, Pa and Ma discovered that they could raise these beautiful melons down there without irrigation. You've heard of the Giulietta watermelons. And the Giulietta watermelons just took a premium all over because they were particularly deliciously sweet. They were not, an irrigated watermelon has a white core in it and is never as sweet. It will have water and it will ripen, but it just doesn't have that special flavor. So in order to have your system all summer long, you start with the first things, and usually as I said, your radishes and your leaf lettuce, not the head lettuce, but the leaf lettuce. And spinach, especially. Spinach was new then. Kids were just learning to eat spinach. And spinach in this country with our rich soil, they'll just grow up and have that beautiful, nice, great, big crinkly leaves. I didn't know that spinach had little old thin leaves until after we left the ranch. Ours were always big, thick, crinkly ones. And then the other thing that made it real good for us was because Raleigh, being a good salesman, went to the PFI camps at Bowbell. And they were so thrilled to get this right fresh from the farm. So we had special days to go up there and special days that Raleigh went to Pullman, Moscow, through Troy, Deere, and all of these little towns. And sold our vegetables. And they were always fresh. We picked them one day and took them early the next morning because we would take that five ton load and go out and Raleigh would sell it wherever he could. And then other people, of course, commensurate too. The cockroaches, you know, raised the watermelons. And other people were raising the watermelons. Now the Gibbs have taken over some of it. I don't think they're doing as good a job as we are because they didn't keep their own seed. And they irrigate. They have to irrigate. Or they think they have to irrigate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You kept your own seed?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yes, we kept our own seed. Because we found them particularly delicious. I remember the muskmelons were about like this. And one year there was a muskmelon that looked like a pumpkin. It had kind of ridges in it. And it was about that long. Oh, possibly this big around. It was the biggest one I ever saw. And I said to Raleigh, I said, We saved. We all saved that seed. It had thick meat like this. And it was just, oh, it was just melting your mouth. It was better than watermelon. Or better than ice cream. And Raleigh looked at it and he said, Well, I don't know honey. He said, I'm not sure that we could pile them up. See, you pile cantaloupes just in crates. And the watermelons you just pile one on top of the other. So, but we raised some. And sure enough, you couldn't pile those things. They'd even collapse with their own weight. Because they were too big. They were just too big.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: So, we'd put them in a special place on the load. And Raleigh would unload them first.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Brings back a lot of things that we did. But this is the way that you did. And we did save our own seed because it was expensive. For vegetables as well as for the melons. The corn was always good. Corn was hard because Raleigh would not pick the sweet corn while it was hot. And so the morning that we left, we always had to get up while it was still dark. And go into the fields and pick that corn then. So that it would be fresh and cold. We didn't have any refrigerators in those days. All we had was ice boxes, you see. And we did have an ice house where we would cut the ice and put the blocks of ice in on the sawdust. But you couldn't put it on a load of melons. You couldn't do it because it just wasn't feasible. So, you had to learn as you went along. And we saved the melons that were the firmest and the hardest. And I remember when we first got the ones that had the thicker. Nowadays you get a certain melon like the New Yorker. And it has a hard yellow thing. You don't get it clear down to the green rinds. You see there's a little place like this. Well, I remember when we first run across those that Raleigh said, oh, he says, I won't sell those. He said, people can't eat that hard stuff. We couldn't. But it did protect what you could eat. And so then we had to just not sell them by weight anymore. We sold them by the piece. Because after all, you can't expect people to pay an awful high price for it. Sometimes they did later because they particularly wanted some special ones. But it's constant change. You have to work with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many people growing truck gardens when the Albrights started here?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: When we were making it, well, then they commenced to. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were pretty much the first men in the area?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, the Raleigh's people were Raleigh's father and mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they started, did they have any examples to follow?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: No, I don't think so. I don't know, Sam, really. I never thought about... They were well in it when I came along. And we fell into it because it was one of the things Raleigh could do when he found that he couldn't keep up with his mechanic thing. He was slated to take over the garage at the Albright garage there, you see. But it didn't turn out that way. And I thought that would be wonderful because, gee, we just have the kids at home and get their education right there. It just didn't turn out that way. But it's okay. And you talked about romance. And I don't think there was romance exactly, except any romance that would come from having your own home. I like to talk about the satisfaction of accomplishment rather than romance. It's bound to be romantic because I can remember when Raleigh made his first thousand dollars that first year. He couldn't wait to come home and tell me. He put the money in the bank up there, you see. See, that was always good. You see, he'd get back and put the money in, then he wouldn't have to bring it home unless it was after banking hours. And he just had to call me and say, well, I put a thousand dollars in the bank today. And so that's romance in a way. But it's still a satisfaction of accomplishment. A man and a woman starting together to found a family and to build a home. And I don't know if anything's more romantic. Really. You set yourself. I've still got this 31st chapter of Proverbs. And there were times when Raleigh and I, Raleigh would be tired, maybe he'd snap me off, you know. And I'd think, well, gee, that's kind of tough, you know. That's kind of tough. And the first time, I was pretty close to tears because I thought, well, then I thought, well, poor guy, you know, he's kind of half sick. So I thought, well, and I can't remember what I did, but it was something that I fixed for supper that he wanted. Oh, yes, it was. I remember now. It was green pea soup.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I fixed the soup and it was hotter than old Hades, you know, and he said, well, what'd you make soup for?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I said, because I thought you were so tired and so worn out, honey, that you'd lost your equilibrium and your good nature and you'd better have some soup. And then he'd start laughing, you know, and he'd come over and he'd spank me and he'd say, yeah, and then he'd kiss me and he'd say, that's the way. So we made our enrollments as we went along. I mean, you know, one of those things. Then when we lost the baby, Raleigh didn't want to have any children.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And, gee, I just realized that, of course, I was 20 when I was married and this I was about it was a third year. And I thought, well, gee, if we're going to have children, we better have it before we're too old. Raleigh is eight years older than I am. And Raleigh wasn't very well. And so I talked him into let's let's have a baby. So we embarked on the baby. And of course, I was walking over there. And when the when I came into labor, it was because of a shock. I like to think of it as a shock, but we found out the baby was a blue baby. He was wound up in the cord. And my labor pains started in the middle of the night. And Raleigh got so excited that he ran off and left me over there and went over to get his father with a lantern. There was that boat and things. I had to get along and I had to walk this half mile on the trail. And Raleigh thought I probably was going to drop that baby right now. You know, one of those things. And so Pa came over and Pa was more excited than Raleigh. He says, good girl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, didn't you know you're going to have this baby?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And I said, no, I didn't. So they got me over there and the baby was. Yeah. Yeah. And and I'm telling you this because I wasn't sure the baby was born and he was a boy, a beautiful boy, but the heart never, never. He was strangled. He was a blue baby and I had uremic poisoning. And if I had been home where my grandmother was, I would have realized that. But Pa Ma did not know. She she she didn't have any daughters of her own and she didn't realize. And I just thought I got big and fat and bloody, but I didn't realize that I had this uremic poisoning. And so when the baby came, why, Dr. Rothwell worked. Oh, how he worked. I'll never forget because the sweat just poured off him. But the baby after that first little squeal and he had a little bowel movement, but he never was conscious. I mean, he just and the doctor, as I say, there wasn't anything left undone that he could have done that he didn't do. And finally, in despair, after two hours of working with the baby, he just says, I was there, of course, on the bed and they were working right right there in the bedroom. And Mollibright was running for cold water and hot water and all this sort of thing, you know, and Pa and Raleigh were in the in the living room there. And Raleigh was in and out of the room, but he couldn't bear to see. He'd never had any experience with birth, you know. And of course, the pains are pretty bad and they didn't know how to alleviate it much in those days. And you just tough it out. And so anyway, after the baby was declared dead, everybody went out and it was about morning then. So they decided that they would have breakfast and have you and I laid there and Raleigh didn't come to me. And I thought, oh, gee, I just wonder, you know, just what he thought. I just didn't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I'll is that turned off? Should it be?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well, this is for your ears only. I was laying there looking at the window. It was it had to do with these kidneys. The vertebra was dislocated, took it over a railroad fence, a gate, and landed sideways. And it was one of those things. And he could no longer lay on the concrete. Now they don't lay on the concrete anymore when they're doing this mechanical work. They have these boards on the wheels and things. But in those days, they didn't. But Raleigh was past that anyway. He was down here. And we went to Troy and work that summer then after the baby. After we lost the baby, we we went up there and stayed one summer. And he worked in the garage there to get our winter supplies. And then after that, why, we were on our own.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did that bother him when he was working here?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yes. Lots of times he would go, he would work himself down and go to bed with something that acted a lot like flu. But it was the kidney thing that he had. And he'd be in bed, oh, two or three weeks maybe until he rested up, got better. So we had to work with it all our lives together. But he's be 84 this fall and he's still going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you become more and more involved in the vegetable business? Like did Raleigh's folks eventually retire? Is that what happened when you took over?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: We took over because, I mean, Raleigh was a good salesman and the other boys were not interested. Clay, the boy next to him, went to the service, went back into the Navy during the war, you see. They needed steam experts in the Coral Islands and way off over there. And so, he never was a farmer anyway. And Fred went to the service and Marv was the younger boy and married and was kind of farming the bigger ones up there. But we, Raleigh used to go to bed real ill, but my children were growing up. We finally had the four, two girls and two boys. God was kind to me and gave me the ones I wanted. Well, you told me, mentioned to me that the kids helped quite a bit in the business and I wanted to ask you about that, how that worked. We worked as a family, all of us. The children went with us from the time and worked, has worked right along with us. We couldn't put them into the field as long, you know. And so, after we moved down here on our tenth wedding anniversary. And this is when we really went into the bigger business because we were buying the fields that were close here and the fields across. We developed those fields over there. They were just little patches. And we knew that we had to get our kids through college, you know. And so, that's when we went into the turkey business. But let's stay to the, we'll stay on the market garden business right now. And we did very well. The youngsters went to the, we didn't need the extra money until the kids got into high school and out. Well then, when my son James graduated from high school, he got out just in time to go to war. They took him, you see, because he, and he was in the Air Force, but he was in personnel. He made his, you'll have to talk to him sometime about how he got along in France. He couldn't talk French and it was up to him, you see, to get all this. That's another story. And then Roland, who was our youngest, and he went, he came to us and he wanted to volunteer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was in 1917 and I said, well, why honey? Why do you want to?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: He says, I want to join the Navy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And again, I said, why?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: He says, Mother, he said, I hear these terrible stories. We lost quite a few of the young men that were in the first of that war around Lewiston and they died in bloody foxholes in France, you know, and things like that. And he says, I'd rather be on a ship. I don't, I just, I don't think I could take it, having to face up to the possible death in a bloody, muddy foxhole. And so we listened. Raleigh looked at me and I looked at him and I said, well, let's listen. Because we didn't want him to run away or desert or anything like this. So we let him, we let him volunteer. And so he went through the war in the Navy and was happy and fortunate. He went through the battles, but was very fortunate. The oldest daughter had been teaching school and that came along and, and she and her young man decided they wouldn't get married. That's her husband and now George. And George had come into the Lewiston area as a special mechanic down here in the glass business. So, they didn't get married. They weren't going to have any children and get married until after the war. But the younger, we started up to the hole one early one morning and the kids had had some friends out. That's one thing. The house was always open because we never knew I'd come down from the field and find anywhere from 10 to 15 to 25 people there for supper, dinner, breakfast or whatever. And I had forgotten the file. The kids files. Yeah. And others too, I mean. So anyway, I forgot my file and I came back to get the file and our youngest daughter and her young man were crying and holding hands and just weeping and Marjorie was just sobbing. She was just the younger, younger girl. And after I busted in on them, why you can't just turn around and walk off, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so I went up and I put my hand on their shoulders and I said, well, dears, what do what's the trouble? You know, can you tell me? Can you can you tell me?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I've never encroached on the kids. I mean, after all they have their own lives. Marjorie says, yes, she says, Bob is going because he can't do special operations training. He has to go to school and we want to get married. We know you won't let us. So we fooled them and let them. And they were just kids. Robert was just barely 19. Marjorie was wasn't yet 18. We let him get married and she followed her husband all over the world. He was sent. His was the security, meaning the information, you know, security part. And he was sent all over from Germany. She earned her singing. She graduated with voice in Germany because they were over there and they had a chance. And these are our two kids that never had any children. And Jean and George were different because they took George in the battle of all those coral islands and the atolls and everything over there and kept him for five years. And when he came home, when he came to rest at the Honolulu, if she had been married to him, she could have gone. But she wasn't married to him. Let me ask you this about the kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were raising them, did you feel that you gave them a lot of leeway or that you had to be fairly strict with them?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: No.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Raleigh was much more lenient. We worked as a family and we treated them as the special members. And I told, as I've often said to the youngsters, I said, you know, you're my jewels, honey. I said, you were the particular reason for of my life, Raleigh and I getting married. And I said, we wanted the children. We got James and Peggy Jean and we were so thrilled with them that we decided to have two more. And God was kind. And we've got another little girl. And I ordered her just like her father and she got everything except his curly hair. And it wasn't nice that we can send her down and have her hair curled. We used to make fun of it like this. And then when Ron Roland came along, I worked him to death the summer before he was born. And he always was a little he was a big baby, but he was he was weaker than the others because the kids had. I didn't realize what I was doing at the time, but the kids had whooping cough. I told him not to play with the kids across the street because they had whooping cough. But so they met. They didn't. They used to invite him over the yard and I said, don't bite them over until they're through. So they met on the railroad track and then my kids got whooping cough anyway. So I was up half the night with kids vomiting. Then I had to get up at three and four o 'clock and go and dig potatoes and what have you. I mean, we just had to. And so the youngsters fell into this family pattern. Because they wanted to. Raleigh very often took me at my word and I'd say, well, the time to teach a youngster something is when he wants to learn. Well, sometimes kids want to learn before they're really able, if you know what, like driving the trucks. Now, he let that young daughter of ours drive a ton and a half truck from here up to the top of that field at five years old when she couldn't even sit. And he said, well, she wants to. And so he showed her and she did it. I mean, and so the children have always have always been a part of the family. And another thing that Raleigh did that got him into trouble with some parents. When the children went to school, see, they were going to high school in Lewiston.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell him about this? That he gave them a checking account of them having their own money?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well, and the children had the privilege from the time they were 10 years old to write checks when it was necessary. And they didn't often abuse it because when those checks came back every month, we went through it as a family. I mean, and so I don't, they just grew up by osmosis, I guess. I don't know. I used to tell them that honesty is the best policy and all this sort of thing, you know, and then sometimes I could point out where others had made a mistake by not being honest that they got into it. I said, well, you remember that we've always said that this is a family policy. And I don't know. It's just one of those things, honey. It's just, they just grew up in the family sometimes. Getting back to the leniency, when James, this is our oldest son, was, maybe I told you this too, started to date, why, he was going around to musicians and oh gosh, he got girls Lord knows where. And he'd take the school, the Chevrolet, we were Chevrolet people because we could afford that, and two or three times I'd get up at three and four o 'clock in the morning and he wasn't even home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was he, you know? What was he doing out there? And I would debate, should I question him or should I not? And finally I talked to him with Raleigh and I said, Raleigh, what do you think?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And he says, well, let me handle this. He says, I'll handle it. So I didn't know for a long time what he did about it. And I found out afterwards that he looked at the car and gee, sometimes it was all spattered up with mud and sometimes the tank was pretty empty, there wasn't any gasoline in it, and there was supposed to be enough to take him a week schooling. And so Raleigh did a lot of this and I never asked, I didn't ask. But then sometimes when James would say, well, I'm going to go up to get Corley, well, this meant up almost to Grangeville and in those days, well, that was a far piece for a high school kid to go on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I'd say, well, have you checked with Daddy?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And sometimes he'd say, well, not yet or I will.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it was the only car we had and this sort of thing, you know?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: So I don't know.
