My youngest daughter, when she got overseas, see, she followed this husband all around and she was thrown into an awful lot of things and she was horrified at some of the things that went on. And finally, her defense, Robert, as Robert says, he was our first son -in -law and he always calls me his favorite mother -in -law, the only one he's ever had, his favorite mother -in -law. So he told me this story and some of the people were talking and they said, she'd say, well, we're not that kind of people. We just don't know anything about it. We're not that kind of people. It's one of those things. And besides, kids are smart. They pick up.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I remember one incident in Roland's, in James's, when I talked to James or to the girls, I could talk right straight from the shoulder. But when Roland came along, he couldn't bear, like, the sex education. Raleigh left that up to me entirely. I don't know what he told. They would ask him questions, maybe, especially the boys would ask him questions. And Raleigh would, he was supposed to be very much the boy about town at Moscow. And so he could speak with a voice of authority. But Roland couldn't talk to me like we are. So we wrote notes to each other. I told him one time when he was about eight years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you done your job?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well, no. Well, it's a nice job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you want me to go?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: No, no. I was just going to say it's half past twelve.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you like to have some lunch?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Oh, I don't know that I should.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you getting hungry yourself?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: No. Well, maybe we could just finish up. But I should probably then get going because I got some stuff for you this afternoon. Okay. But I was going to ask you about what happened during the Depression, if that became a difficult time for you and your business. Yes, it was. But Raleigh, we had quit the market gardening, you see, then and we were in the turkey business. And the turkey business gave us more money, cash, because we had to get our kids through, that was the first depression, we've had several.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And we managed. The grains, you know, were not as expensive. The farmers weren't doing as well because after all, it was depressed. And when the grain was, the price was depressed, that was good for us because it made our feed more logical and we could do this. And I was the geneticist and we saved our own, we developed, I put 18 years of my life in developing our own broad -breasted bronze and they were wonderful turkeys, I've told you about this. We haven't put any of that on tape and I would like to know how you went about developing the grain. I had in mind what I wanted and because we belonged to this Idaho Poultry Association, I had the University of Idaho, they didn't have a turkey flock and they wished to establish that polorum vaccine. And so, Pren Moore was at the head of the poultry department at the time and when he found out that I was eager to learn, he was eager to teach. And so, he told me that I would have bone troubles getting those heavy birds on a straddled foot, you know, bone trouble. But I didn't because Raleigh was the feed man, you see, he mixed all the feed and went through those formulas. That wasn't my baby, mine was the geneticist, the physical turkey that I wanted. And instead of a high one like this, the breastbone, I wanted a low one, those broad this way. And we ran into a couple but because Pren Moore was there and then finally Mr. Lampman who is now retired too, you see, we went through all of that rigmarole there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At the university?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, at the university. They gave me a liberal education and I was getting broad bones and beautiful things but I was getting a pinhead and I didn't have sense enough, I didn't know about it. And Pren Moore came down and he looked at these turkey hands and he said, oh, he says, Laura, that will never do because you're developing your big beautiful structure at the expense of your energy. And he says that life energy is what you've got to keep.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I said, what do I do?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And he said, well, and he went through the block and he looked a little bit and he says, here. He says, this is one of the best heads I've had and it was a round full head, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he says, do you see the difference in the color?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And I could see that that hen had full blood up there and these pipsqueak ones, while they were broad, they wouldn't have laid the eggs and the eggs would not have had the vitality, the vitality. I had to learn these things. And because we belong to the association and because I was trying to do something that he enjoyed, he was an old man at the time. And so every time he had a chance, he'd drop by and see how it was getting along. And then they brought their people and some of them were teachers and some of them were special class people down to work in our selection because they were learning on our flock, you see. And by cooperating this way, then I had the background of this university and I did a real good job with their help.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made you decide to develop your own breed rather than going with what was already on the market?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: The long lanky high, let me digress just the moment and say that they were big, big birds. But an ordinary woman's oven, of course, we were selling to the hotels, could manage a hen. But some of those turkeys were that high and they're too close. They were burning, you know, on this. And besides, they weighed heavy, but they didn't look pretty. They just didn't look pretty. And we were in a highly competitive, the Wrights were doing this, raising turkeys down there on the Snake River. They started with some of ours and then they got pulled from the east and I don't know about all they went into it. They didn't make as well as we did because they had to hire an awful lot of help. But I decided that I wanted to do this and I worked on it. I studied, I went to the conferences and we took, I could, gee, I wonder what I did with them. I was going to say, we took them to the fairs and put them up against the, and we used to get a lot of these purple ribbons, you know, one thing and another. I wonder what I did with all those. I bet you they're in the attic over at the old house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So was it a continuous process of crossbreeding that you were involved in? Well, we had a lot of things to do because they, have you ever seen a tom breed?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: You know, they just dance on those poor old hens and scratch them in their claws. And so we put saddles on them. I was the first one to make a saddle. I said, Raleigh, we've got to do something about this. So we made turkey saddles out of canvas. We put the wings on them, you know, and then so that not to tear those hens when those hens are all, backs are all torn up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You put the saddle on the hens?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: On the hens, uh huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would they like, the saddles?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well, they got used to it, let's say. They didn't like it at first, but I made them like, kind of like this and then the holes, you see, for the wings to go through. We used to, we tried to tie them on at first. Well, this would cut into their wings, you see. And a poultry, a poultry skin is a delicate skin, whether you believe it or not. But those toms, and there's no way, a tom just doesn't do any breeding without a treading. Well, after he slips off a hen's back two or three times, those claws, he's got her ripped open and she, you can't expect her to eat and feel good and lay eggs and... Was there a lot of leather that you made to... No, I made them out of canvas for two reasons. The toms had to be able to, and I put ridges on them, they had to be able to find security there while they're treading these hens. And the poor hens are laid out there, stressed out with their necks and the toms are having a terrible time too. And then to actually come into contact, they have to be standing on here with a hold of the feathers on that hen's neck in order to get the two... ...vocalas together in order to fertilize. Well, it's quite a procedure. So, I would make about 385 new saddles every year because they just wore them out. But it protected the hens and then we could sell them. You couldn't sell, I mean, they would just be slitted and torn. And as I said, to Raleigh, I says, Heaven's honey, you can't expect those hens legs when they're sick and they're worn and torn like that. And so...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what was the size and the scope of the operation when you built it up? I mean, how many did you have?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: We tried for 20 ,000. I had the old incubators, I hatched them myself in the basement there because we found that when we bought the poles, you never knew what you were getting. You just didn't know what you were ever getting. And as I said, these big, big heavy ones, an ordinary farm wife couldn't get the toms in her oven. She could get hens, but the hens, of course, were more expensive than the toms. So I devolved the wide, the wide breast, the low breast bone, but put the meat out here and on the drumsticks, a big drumstick. Our drumsticks were big like this. And then the feet, we had very little, very little. I didn't... Prenmore helped me with this. When I got this vitality message, I didn't have any more bone trouble. And so we got along very well. And then I went to the legislature and it was about this time that the kids had come home from the war and we were just through... Roland still had... could have used some more money to go to school, but because the war ended and he had been an officer, you see, those GI benefits helped. And so he went to school on GI benefits. But about that time, the price broke and the farmers grain came back into being. And the last two years we just made expenses. We didn't make any profits. And it isn't fun to do all of that hard work, you see, unless you can make a profit. And so when I was in the legislature, I was appointed in 1949 and it was fun. I enjoyed it. It's quite a story behind that too. And while I was down there, the boys sold my... We kept 500 hens as a seed flock. And they had a chance to sell those beautiful big hens and they sold them all off, all but 19. And oh, it really gave me quite a start because I'd put 18 years of my life and I said, well kids, that's it. I said, there won't be any more turkeys. And they had just come home from the war and I thought they were through with the ranch, but they'd like to continue it, but they sure weren't happy with a lot of your profits. And they didn't want to go through all of that. So that was that. And we raised, I would hatch 20 ,000 and we'd usually raise anywhere from 10 to 15 ,000. And we'd start selling them, Sam, as soon as they were frying size. And this would give us... usually those hens would start laying real early in March and April. Well, it takes four weeks to hatch them, you know. And then it would take three to four months to get them up to four or five pounds frying size. But that was a nice one. And so it brought in some money. But when the price of feed went up, when the farmers got back into the... it wasn't much fun. So anyhow, 1949, the kids sold my hens. I said, okay, that's it. So they bought poults for a couple of years after that. But the people that had had standing orders, we had people that we'd been selling turkeys to for 20 years, you know, just on standing orders. They just knew when Thanksgiving time would come, they could always get whatever they wanted here. And so they'd just send me a postcard and say, well, we want such and such and such and such. And that was it. So when we sold out, they had to get them some place else. But it had served its purpose. And Raleigh was ill, yeah. He got to the place where he couldn't mix the feed because of the dust that was there. And I had never operated it and the kids hadn't either. And I would have stayed if the children had been interested in it and all. But you see, they had come up in their late teen years when everything was just going great guns fine. Well, then when the problems commenced to develop, well, that wasn't fun. So they just simply sold my hens off. I said, okay, that's it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: During the years when it was going fine, did it require full day's work to take care of them?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Oh, my, yes. We had housekeepers all the time. The children took care of their own bedrooms, the girls. And then we all helped with, oh, there were general housework, the laundry and this. But we had a housekeeper. We had to have a housekeeper because I couldn't be in the basement taking care of all those eggs in that hatchery. And then when the poles were first put out, you see, you have to be real careful with them. And there's many problems there that was funny. For instance, the eggs, we got an electric one. Raleigh thought an electric incubator was the thing. So we had a 1200 electric incubator that we put in down there. And I thought, oh, boy, this is wonderful because instead of bringing the trays out, you just turn them off and you could cool them. And then there was they were on a big, big swinging drum in there and you just turned it, you know, turn the whole 1200 all at once. Why, gee, life was not like this. But they didn't hatch. They didn't hatch like they should. There was cripples there. There there there was other things like drowning. They didn't they didn't evaporate off like they should. Or if you shut that off, they would get too dry and stick. And they they there were cripples. And I knew that cripples was was lack of turning. So finally, I evolved a a system and I would put them into the electric incubator for the first two weeks and just turn them. You see, that's before the chick actually is starting very much. The yolk has not been absorbed and all this sort of thing. And then I would take them out of there and put them into the wide, big, big 400 egg trays. And you take them out every morning and then you just roll them with your hands. And there was something about the actual rolling of the of the hands because it wasn't the same one all the time. It's more like the hen would do it. And then they had the the aerators and the moisture pans there. And I had better luck by hatching them in these. And so it was more work. But and the thing that I found, this is one of the things that always kind of amazed me and tickled me. Naturally, you're talking when you're doing this. And those chicks and those eggs got and I was putting the two came to my voice. I would put them out in the Bruder house and I'd be talking and they'd all rush over and pile up. And Turkey can commit suicide more ways anyway in the corners.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I thought, well, what's the matter with them?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I know a Turkey is crazy, but they're not as crazy as that. And this old Mr. Lampman was the new was the new poultryman down there. And so I said, come on down and see. I really thought there was something about it with those poles, because we had the three big Bruder houses there and they were acting the same and every Bruder house. So he came down, he went around with me and finally he's there. Forget that post and he slapped his done and he came in to laugh. He said, they're coming to your voice. He says, you keep your mouth shut and see. And by golly, that was it. When I would talk out there, they were so used to it. In the egg, it never occurred to me, Sam, that this was it. In the egg itself. Because I would be talking to these people, you know, rolling those eggs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, who'd think of them?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And yet there I was, right, talking to them. And they so you learn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are turkeys really dumb animals?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: No, but they are.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to say tradition written, but I'm thinking of what what would you say?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Nature, the nature of a turkey there. And they're very clever. They're very clever. But it's like any wild animal, I guess. They they I learned about this, about the chicks anyway. They you see that that voice was was the mother voice. And so, of course, when they were in that strange Bruder house, why, if I was speaking, why they were piling over there trying to get to mother.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: A turkey does what a what a turkey is supposed to do in spite of heck and high water. I've seen them swim a slough. And oh, Sam, we take them on the range and we'd, for instance, a crossover with big trees over there. And this was the we'd want them to drink out of the creek eventually, you see, they'd have to drink out of the creek. So we would have the water and we would put them over there with first roost. They would learn to go to roost here before we put them over there and then to stay by the feeders, because on the end of these runs that we had out here, well, here was always the feeders, you see, and the water things. So they were they were taught to stay by the feeders. Well, this is fine. But when you want to put them to roost, you had to teach them to go to roost. So I'd go over and sit on those roost and make a noise like a turkey hen and get them to come up in about a week. Then they'd go. They were supposed to go to roost and they like to roost. And then maybe I'd leave a little bit too early before it was real plumb dark. I'd come across the river and the first thing I'd hear, they were all flying after me and falling into the river and going down the river.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do about that?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well, the first time or two we lost some. I mean, some of them would get up on the bank. They can swim. They can just swim almost like a duck. But after they'd fall in there and two or three fall on top of them, well, last they'd get their feathers wet, you know, and then they'd sink. So we lost some naturally because we had to. Then the golden eagles came down after them. We'd have the children up with them and those golden eagles would come down, you know, and great big birds that would stand that tall, you know, land out there and get after our poor turkeys. And then they'd all flush into the brush and pile up and break their legs and wings flying into trees and stuff and in panic. And I could write a book not about me and the egg, but me and turkeys.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I thought I would once and then I thought, oh, to heck with it. This is over and I'll tell you what. I understand that, but how did that... I'm curious about this person, Christianity. And I understand it is in a way it's their own form. It's not exactly like... No, that isn't true. No, no. The basic... the Methodist over here was an offshoot. Let me talk with my mouth full. You listen with yours full.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I first became aware of it because we had a South Methodist Sunday school down here at Arrow and that's where we went to church because we had to work. And oh, for a long time I had a real guilt complex because I had been taught, I had been raised in this Methodist home. And you just don't work on Sunday, plan to work on Sunday. And we had to work on Sunday. All of us had to work on Sunday. Be getting these loads ready to go out early Monday morning. And so I would compromise with dear old God and the Lord Jesus Christ and a few others because we'd go up and we'd do all this work and then we'd rush home and have breakfast. And I made a point to always take the youngsters to Sunday school. And Raleigh knew that this was the way it was. And he would stay home and pack those cantaloupes. And we had them by the tons and there'd be maybe as many as 200 or 250 crates of cantaloupes. Well, you don't pack those, just out. And I never, I didn't have the eye to do it. Raleigh had this mechanical eye and he could just look and say, this is a 36, this is a 27 and they'd have two or three crates going, you know, and this is a 48 and he'd pick up a cantaloupe and it'd just fit right in there. And I had to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean, 30? Is it size in inches?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, no, yeah, so many to the crate. So many to the crate. A 27 was about this big around, a 36 would be about this big and then they go down to this big. And he could tell by just looking at the darn things that I had to pick them up and try to fit them in there. I didn't have that mechanical thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you went to Sunday school with the kids?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And so I'd go take the kids and go to Sunday school and Raleigh would be packing those cantaloupe in the shade. That was the only good thing. We'd have our breakfast. And then we'd come home and Jeannie learned she had this same thing from her father. She could pack. And she would pack. And then I would take the younger youngsters and go up. We would have these watermelons picked early and piled by the road because we had to pack, we had to load some of the crates of cantaloupes to make a corral to put the watermelons in, you see. And then we had to put some of the watermelons in before we could get up to get the rest. It was a long thing and we worked all day Sunday. And so the Gibb's was a retired minister in the South Methodist Church. And he was our minister down here at Arrow.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And during that time, the missionary, the mission to the Methodist Church in Lapway had fallen by the wayside and Mrs. Gibb's was supposed to be that lay missionary over there. Well, she just was so old and so weak and so tired that she couldn't do it. So I sort of fell into that. And that's how I, I never had any training for missionary or anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did, what was missionary, lay missionary work like?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I would go over there. They had their meeting in them in the afternoon because there was a lot of those Indians that came from far away. I played the piano. I led the singing. I taught whoever was in the choir was in the adult class. And so I had a very fine mixture. Some, a lot of Presbyterians was not going to the Presbyterian Church because they had a native minister. And my Methodist, Pacific Northwest South Methodist group helped me buy the materials like song books, study material, quarterlies. And then we organized a women's group, a women's organization there. And I taught them quilting and they're real clever with their hands anyway. And then they taught me beadwork and we had these sales that would help to undergird the work. I never did get you your cheese.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you did?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I don't need it. I'm sure. Oh yeah, this is fine. Well, I'm going to have some.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And because it was a South Methodist outfit, we weren't in connection with the North Methodist group and they were, the South Methodists were not as active in this neighborhood as they had been in earlier years. And so I just did what anybody would do at a Sunday school. I, if I, and we had two preachers, two local preachers. One was Steven Rubin, an old fellow, and the other was Star J. Maxwell. And Star J. Maxwell was a man that had started, had been converted under the Catholic Church and had started to be a priest and got waylaid as a young man because, and Steven Rubin too, because they took to drinking. And a drop of alcohol in an Indian just doesn't work. It just doesn't work. They can't handle alcohol at all. And Steven Rubin, before I came on, had been suspended by the South, well, the Presbyterian Church it was. And he could no longer preach in the Presbyterian Church and he was just way out there. And because of his basic conversion, and I, this I'd like to, you said it was maybe not as deep or that they had converted it to their own nature worship and so on, great spirit worship. But this wasn't true. Steven Rubin was truly converted to Christianity. And the first time that I saw him in a conference was at Troy, Idaho.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he was asking, petitioning to be a lay preacher, meaning that that he didn't go to school and have a theological be, what do you call it?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Whatever it is, you know, the degree. Yes. And, but he was licensed by the Pacific Northwest Conference at these Methodist meetings. He could do that. I could have had a license if I wanted to because I had the gift of gab, but I didn't, I didn't want to. But Steven wanted that. And there stood that old Indian man. And he, he was, he was up there giving his side of the versions. And he had been hurt and I don't know what all. And he drank and he got drunk and all of this thing. And that's why his license had been suspended, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was giving his side of the story?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And he was, yeah, and he was asking, this was his petition to get, to be licensed again. And then we would all vote whether we should license him or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But this would be as a Methodist?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, South Methodist Church. And he was, he was quitting. He was petitioning in the South Methodist Church because the Presbyterians, you see, he was too proud to ask him. I think they would have taken him back. And it was Dar J. Maxwell had gone to a preschool.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But, uh, did he get back in? Was he ordained?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, he wasn't ordained, but he was licensed. And he became a Methodist minister. And he could preach in the Methodist and was recognized. This was especially the recognized. He could have preached in that church over there if he didn't want to anyway, but he couldn't be connectional, you see. He couldn't be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he stopped drinking?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah. But, and I thought, well, gee, I just felt like crying too. Poor old fellow. And there he was and his heart was so broken. And then when I went to, when Mrs. Gibbs died and no longer could do it, they didn't have anybody. And so Charlie White, now Charlie White was an old baseball player. Ball player, I've heard of him. A darn good one. Yeah. And he played with Raleigh's brother, Fred. And through them, Charlie White knew that, that I played the piano and sang and might come over and help them with their Sunday school. So they come and ask me and I said, well, yes, I can. And I said, well, Charlie, I'll tell you what, I don't play the piano very well. I can't play that good. But I says I can play the hymns if you'll come and lead the singing. Because I said, I can't lead the singing very well with my back to the audience. So he says, OK, by golly, I'll do it. So we did it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And for about 14, 15 years, why, I went over every afternoon and if I got home before dark, Raleigh would say, oh, what's the matter? The Indians mad at you?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Because, because I was there, we had the Sunday school and then we had the church every Sunday. And in the morning, I'd take my kids. We'd go down to the arrow or wherever it was and had it. But I fell into it that way and I did this. And then I, because I was district director of the Methodist Women, why, they knew I was doing that and they would send us aid. They'd even come and buy some of the things. We started the dinner. The Indians loved to eat. And we'd have all these people. And the Christmas, gosh, the only Christmas that some of those people had was at that church. And they'd always bring this beautiful tree in there and just squander. I just thought it was terrible squander dollars. And let me...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On decorating it?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah. And the things that the big dinner and they'd invite all of their relatives to come over to the house. That white house is close to the church is the parish house. I mean, it isn't where the minister lives, but it's the, it would be like the social rooms of an ordinary church was that house. And they had some, some strict ideas. You didn't, you didn't cavort and sing and raise the devil in the church. You were very circumspect there. That was the Lord's place. And they used to meet me at the door sometimes and they had two little Sunday school rooms. And when I'd see old dear old brother, Stephen Rubin, or then later star J Maxwell waiting for me to come and take me off in one of those rooms to lay the problems on my shoulders. And I'll never forget one of the times that Stephen Rubin was the summertime and our women's meeting was at Leland. That was a South Methodist church too. So I was off up there and had taken a couple. Well, when our Indians die, they are not embalmed or anything, you know, and they get them into the ground. They have to just as fast as they can. Brara won has been the, the people that have taken care of them because, Oh, there's a long story behind that. But anyway, this is one of the things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so they call them and the Brara ones all rush up there and get that body and take it down and what, what's the name?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Brara won the funeral people at Lewiston and just the body, clean it up and get it all ready and then put it in cold storage until, because till they get their funeral arrangements made, take care of it. And sometimes if there's people that have to come from far away, it's maybe a week before they would have the funeral, but otherwise they do it just as quickly as they can because in the old days, they didn't have any embalming and you had to hurry up and get it under cover. Well, this time I was up to Leland and the funeral came and dear old brother, Stephen had to have the funeral services and he had to have somebody play the piano and he didn't know where, see Mrs. Albright was gone. And they always have a big, a big dinner. A funeral always is a big dinner. Always. This is Indian. This is, this is one thing that the Indians do and it is a feast. And the, the, I have seen the clothes. It depends on how, I don't think they would do it anymore now, but this has been 40, 50 years ago. Well, about 40 years ago. And they would bring all the belongings maybe and put them out in the, in the home and they would all go and pick out a shirt or they would pick out a pin or something to remember them by and then come over and have this dinner and a social and then talk about the deceased and how good he was or how bad he was or however, whatever. This is part of it. And sometimes they have a wake. It depends on the family and so on. Well, I wasn't there and I wasn't available. So, the next Sunday when I got ready, after I got home, I found out that they had to have this funeral services and so old Stephen Rubin had, had worked it up and found some singers and somebody to play the piano and had had the funeral services and buried the youngster. It was a young 19 year old, somebody or other, was a relative of one of the families. But when I came home, he met me at the door and I came in and so he didn't say anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He just met me at the door and he took me by my arm and took me into the little room and I said, well, what is it, Stephen? I said, what's, what's, you know, what's bothering you? What's your problem? He says, where were you last Thursday? And I said, and I kind of opened my mouth and he says, where were you when I needed you?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And then it dawned on me. And I said, Stephen, you know where I was. I said, I was at Leland and I said, you had the funeral services, didn't you, for the, for the McCormick baby. And he said, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, and where were you when I needed you?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And I said, well, I was doing the Lord's business with the Methodist women at Leland. And I said, don't you know, I had two of our women that were there. And I said, two of our singers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, who did you get to sing? Who played the piano?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: You know, he says, we don't play the piano. We sing Indian style. Wasn't anybody to play the piano. So they sang Indian style. And he said, oh, I just want you to know that you're supposed to be here when I need you. And I said, I know that Stephen, if you had called at Leland, maybe. And he says, I didn't know where to call. Well, he says, anyhow, I needed you and you weren't here. So you better be on the ball. Bless his heart. They put many words in the, in his first language that definitely is based in the Christian religion.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And they, some of them, of course, they can't stand liquor and some of them would get drunk. And when they'd come into church, they would come up in our church. We have this nice big carpet. It's a parlor rug, you know. And we don't, we don't, we don't have, when we're in church, well, then we have the altars. But for Sunday school, it's just a table with a superintendent there and the secretary and the treasurer. So when they have a personal witness and they really, when they give their witness, they really give a witness. I mean, it's so heartfelt. You just ought to experience some time. I just wonder if they still do. And they would come up and stand on the edge of this rug. And pretty soon, Stephen Rubin was the head of the Sunday school in the church at the time. Pretty soon Stephen would look up and he'd say, well, brother Charlie or whoever,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what's troubling you?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And then they will confess whatever it is they have on their soul or make a witness or whatever it is. And if old brother Rubens thinks that it's acceptable, he will walk over and shake hands with this person that is standing on the rug here before God and the congregation. And then turn around, go back and sit maybe one in the pews.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then Stephen would get up and he'd say, are there any others that would like to come forward?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And he didn't say confess, but make a statement is what he would say, you see. Well, maybe some of them would. And maybe it would be, oh, a young girl. And she would come up and say, well, I was over two so and so's and I got mad and I broke her doll or whatever it was, you know. And so then he'd go and put his hand on her shoulder and shake her hand. And if it was some of his own relatives, he might kiss her on the forehead and then send her back. And then no one else would come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So everybody would go back and sit down and then Stephen Ruben would say, anybody have a song?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And everybody had favorite hymns and they weren't a bit backward about talking about it. And if it was a song in the Methodist hymnal, then I would be playing and Charlie would be standing there and be singing. The choir would be singing. Here would be 15 or 20 of the nicest younger Indians singing. And then these people back here, Sam, it was the most interesting thing. They would stand up where they were and maybe two or three would stand up and talk at the same time, which bothered me at first. Until I learned that they weren't concerned about these other people. They were just concerned about talking to God and telling him that they had done thus and so. They were sorry that they had done thus and so or they had been hurt or what have you and asking for a prayer. Now, they could pray themselves. They could ask Stephen to pray. They could ask me to pray. They could ask any of the older Christians to pray. And we had three that were there that had been converted under the leadership of the last missionary that came to the Presbyterian group, you see, because some of them had defected into the South Methodist group. And then the Waters, you remember that Waters and South Methodist went to Kamiyia and had that wonderful sing long, long years ago, long before. Well, some of those, their grandmothers were converted under that Waters outfit. At Kamiyia, long years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was this door? What did you hear about the one at Kamiyia? Was it just for a short period of time?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, it was just a revival. And they and our Indians would have a revival over the Christmas, New Year's for three solid weeks. We just go over there and they would have a regular emotional upheaval. But I learned at first, as I said, two or three people talking at the same time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: During the hymns, during the singing?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Or right afterwards, if they were busy singing, if they were singing in a nest purse, they would be apt to get up and talk anytime. But usually they wanted the minister that was presiding to hear them. I mean, the vindication because they expected that human touch. And unless the person up there heard what they said, you just don't respond. It's one of those things. And so two or three of them might be talking at the same time. I often wondered how Star J. Maxwell, who was hard of hearing. And since I'm getting a little hard of hearing, I've often thought I wonder if he really heard as much as he thought he did. But very sincere, very dedicated. And the young people used to follow Charlie because of the ball playing. But I don't know what's happening there. Then after I was there, we had two trained missionaries. But it wasn't the same. The younger girls, they were trained as missionaries, but honey, I don't know what happened. They looked down their noses exactly, but I would sit there sometimes and I would look at Miss Caravell. Mrs. Smith was an older woman and she was the best one that they had. But the others would just get so bored. Of course, after you sit there for three hours, well, you're liable to get bored anyway. But they don't have their revivals anymore. I don't know. It sounds like perhaps they didn't know the people well enough or weren't in as close as they could. They put the church, the Methodists gave that church. Well, they didn't give it. They asked Henry Sugden, who is now a retired Presbyterian minister that owns a home down there. He was raising a bunch of youngsters. The Presbyterian Indian Church has their own ministers there. Dave Miles does a little preaching. He works at the park service there. But they haven't had a revival meeting for a long, long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the revivals like?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: You said they went on a long time and they were very emotional. I'm wondering what they were like when they had them. It sounds like quite an experience compared to, you know. They would come into the church over the Christmas holidays, you see. They would have preaching every night for two weeks. Sometimes they would go down in the Pendleton, Oregon area because they're close. You see, a lot of our Nesburs, Bill Williams and some of those were raised down in Dayton on the Patahaha River and so on down in there. And so, they're close to those Indians. And they would exchange pulpits maybe. And our Indians would go down there. Then when we'd have our revival, they'd come up here. And I belong to a Soroptimus Club, which is a service club for women, you know. It's supposed to be policy making women. And as co -owner of Albright's Farms, they invited me. And I've enjoyed it because I enjoy the quality of the women who belong to it. They're all policy making women, usually in their own businesses or in offices of professional men or so on. Well, anyway, for Christmas, they'd say, well, Laura, gee, the Mother's Club, for instance. My kids were in high school. I was asked to join the Mary Ball Mother's Club down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What can we do?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I said, I've got a dandy thing. I said, why don't you come on out to the, maybe you'd like to furnish some of the treats for our Indian, our Indian Christmas. I said, you don't have to come out Christmas Day, but come on out during the holidays. I said, they'll be having some out there all the time. And the women are there. So, they picked it right up and the Mother's Club said, oh, we'll make some popcorn balls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they like that?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And I said, they would love it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how many do we need?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I said, well, kids, because that's the only Christmas that many of those Indian families have, the church is always full. There's about 500 people that come in there for Christmas Eve. So, we would need three, four, five hundred. So, they got to thinking, well, gee, that's about, they figured they could get a hundred into a clothes basket, you know, balls like this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do we need to put strings on them?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I said, no, don't put strings on them. You don't need to because I said, some of you come on out and just stand there and hand it to them as they leave after the service, if you'd like to bring them for the real Christmas. If you'd like to enjoy the music and come because those Indian voices, they can just sing like nobody's business. I tell you. When Star J. Maxwell would get up and sing, Just As I Am, you know, that good old one, boy, he really met everyone because he says, I know I'm sinful, but just as I am, my Lord and God is willing to accept me. And so, oh gosh. So, they would come out and for ten years almost, the Mother's Club got so that it was too much for them. They got older after all. We did get older. So, the Soroptimist kind of took it up and they decided then that it was too much of an effort for their own. And so, they would give a birthday party, a big birthday party in the summertime. And they always go up to Talmex over the Fourth of July. By the way, I'm the only woman who's ever addressed a patriotic discourse to Talmex on the Fourth of July. So, they came up there and instead of popcorn balls, why, they made peanut nuggets or, you know, candy, a whole lot of candy stuff. So, through the years, why, the Indians have had a lot of help. Then, when the Methodists went together, the South and North Methodists, they took over that mission, but it sure fell by the wayside. And then finally, they put it under the management of, which is too bad, Henry Sugden and Henry's design from the whole darn outfit. Presbyterians, Methodists and everything, because he didn't want to be, you know, ministers have to go where you're appointed. And he had his home there and the children were there and so, he just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, I asked my people, when I see them, I said, well, are you having services?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Oh, yes, we meet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And do you have Sunday school?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well, we don't have classes anymore. We just have the lesson and everybody does it. Well, you know what that is. The children get restless and the young folks aren't interested particularly. But, you know, it is an amazing thing now. Charlie White's son, Roy, is now a grandfather. And he grew up in that church, you know, and his kids grew up, but they had a falling away because there wasn't, I don't know what happened. I just don't know. I thought maybe it was a drinking problem, but I don't know. I didn't ask Roy because I thought it was embarrassing to him and I wouldn't. Well, the revival, let me see if they were emotional.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like for those people, the revival, as emotional experience?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Usually a weeping spell. I mean, they were willing. This is the thing to me. I don't know of any White, in fact, I've been going to the Methodist Church up here at Kendrick since, well, it's been about 12, 15 years now. And only once in all that time, Sam, has everybody given what we call a testimonial meeting. White people just don't confess their sins. They don't recognize them. They cover them up. And they would never think of standing up in church and really confessing out loud what they even considered in their own minds that they consider. Because you know very well if you've cheated anybody, you know whether you have lost your temper, you know whether you've sworn and taken, and the Indians would bring this out with weeping. And lots of times I've seen those grown men put their hands up over and cry, just actually cry because they felt that they let God down. Well now, White people just, if they felt that they still wouldn't make any... I learned a lot from them in facing up to what's expected of you, let's say. Well that's interesting. You feel that they really taught you. And I would like to go back. In fact, I thought maybe I would be going back. And then I talked to my daughter and she says, Mother, I think you'd be foolish if you did because you'd get involved again. And there wouldn't be any more church mergers to save you this time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, well what do you mean save me?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And she said, well, remember Mother, she says, my driving restrictions have been restricted to daylight only. And you can't drive after night anymore. And she said it would be just a matter of going to church. And I said, well, I know that. I could at least enjoy being with them over there maybe. I'd join in their worship service and what have you. And she said, well, and I said, as far as you're concerned, I bet you're afraid that you're going to be roped in on it. I said, I won't ask you to. She's a good Presbyterian.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know much about the history of the Methodist congregation there before you became involved? When that church became a Methodist church? And who the early Methodists were there?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: As if they had all the time in the world and not as if they were led by a set law. This is true. And as long as anyone wants to talk, they are courteous enough to listen. And sometimes it's... I finally settled. I mean, I had hens. It depends on when you want to sell them. And at first it was always Thanksgiving Day. And so they have to be six to seven months old. And between six and seven months I had hens that weighed 25 pounds. Average 25 pounds. Sometimes would go up to almost 29. But sometimes those larger, heavier hens was not as good a physical form, you see, as the little plumper ones. But the turkeys would be between 39 and 47 pounds at that 34 weeks of age. And that grows pretty fast, you see. I mean, they would be heavy. That's live weight. Yes, that's live weight. And then we were taking them to Spokane for a long time. And then after the kids got on from the war, they set up Albright's Poultry that you've heard me mention. And that building out there is still Albright's Poultry. It's not kept up anymore, but it's there where the old 18th Street bridge used to come across Lowston. And we thought that our own kids maybe could get the profit that Spokane was getting when we took them live weight up there. We killed a lot here and dry -picked for special orders. But a family can only do so much. And with all the other feeding that we had to do, we could not possibly kill the five or six thousand, you see, that we would have to sell for Thanksgiving. Then the others went for Christmas. And then the hens that had laid the eggs for my hatchery, then I always sold after the first of the year because they would have to go. They were not first grade birds, you see. I mean, they were no longer those first seven, thirty -four, thirty -eight weeks because they'd been a year in the laying hens. And yet here they were, just one year old, and the hens were nice, big hens and good hens. So then we would be killing turkeys probably until, what, February, March, and what have you. Because we wanted to get rid of them. Because then by that time I was starting the new cycle. I would have the... you can't sell your hens until you get the eggs that you want, you see, and get them hatched. And so it was really quite something. And after they get through the laying season, some of them would be scarred and some of them would be hurt and what have you. And they wouldn't be as fat as they would be as young ones, you know. So you have to take second grade and price and sometimes they would grade up and we got so we could tell. But after the boys came home from the war, they established Albright's Poultry down there to get that profit. Then I didn't have to do any more killing out here, you see. We just took them all down there. They took care of them. And then my kids were through college anyway and they didn't want it. And so we just, as I say, when I was away they sold my hens and I said, okay kids, that's it. That's how we got out of the business. When talking about the Indian religion, I was wondering if you could tell me a little more of what Steve Rubin was like. Stephen Rubin, what he was like as a... I've heard of him previously and I have a feeling he's a rather well known figure around the country. A very dedicated old man, a very God -fearing old man. And one time I was in a conference,

speaker: Lora Albright
words: which I don't know if I should tell you this or not, because it was a white conference, I mean a Methodist conference, and so the Indians were up there.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And Stephen Rubin was with me and he was an old man, he couldn't drive, and so I had driven the... it was Troy, I don't mind, it was Troy, Idaho.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And I can't remember just... I do remember this incident because Stephen Rubin had been talking and some of them said, well Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Albright says, can you add anything to that?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I said, no. Stephen has done very well. This is it. We are there and I said the only thing I could mention has been mentioned that the women meet once a month for a study and a worship. And then I says we meet every week to work up the things in the house to keep it clean and we have... and these public dinners, oh land. Everybody comes in and eats and if they feel like it they bring something and if they don't they come and eat anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: We fed 325 people over there and you have to wash dishes all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This is for one public dinner? How often would these be?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well, every quarter, you know, every time you have an Easter and you have Fourth of July and you have Christmas and you have Thanksgiving and you have you name it. And I can't remember just how it came about but anyway I said, well, this is fine. I said, we'll be here and we'd like to have you come back again and we're glad that you came and all of this. And so they turned it back to Stephen Rubin and this is what he said. I mean the white person had made a... well, I don't know, it was an aside let's say. Saying, well, when you get to heaven you'll have a star in your crown for the work that I was doing over there and with the Indians, you know. And I don't know whether Stephen heard her or just what but he got up and he made this remark. He said, we thank God for Mrs. Albright. And he said, you know, she is young woman. His English, he'd leave off his own. She is young woman but someday she die. But probably I die first and I will be up there in heaven and when she comes to the pearly gate I will say to Saint Peter, you let her in. She's a good woman, you know. And sat down. Well, there I sat with my thumb in my mouth practically, you know, this and those white people just roared and I was so embarrassed because the idea. And so everybody looked at me, you know, and I thought, oh Lord help me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so I got up and I walked up there and while I was walking I thought, now what can I say?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And the Lord gave me this idea. So I said, when I get up there I said, well thank you Stephen. I said, I appreciate this because I said, I do appreciate your remarks. And then I turned to the audience and I said, and as far as I'm concerned having worked with Stephen about five years now, if Stephen Rubin will do this for me when I get to heaven I'll be very happy to have him sponsor me when I get there. And I meant it. I mean I really meant it because he was just as sincere as he could be that when I get up there I hope he's there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he in fact the minister of the church during that period of time?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: No, well, he was a Presbyterian minister and then because of his... Right, but as a Methodist... No, he was a licensed lay preacher but would never be a member of the conference because that would mean that he could be sent to Oregon or wherever, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he was the one who presided over most of the services, right?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: No, Star J. Maxwell was there. Oh, I see. At the same time. Star J. Maxwell was a different sort. Now he worked at the agency and he was very intelligent and while I think he was a dedicated Christian he didn't have that... Well, I was going to say total dedication that dear old Stephen Rubin had but that's not true either because Star J. Maxwell could have gone ahead and been a Catholic priest. But he had a vision that he used to tell about and it's said that that was not for him, that he was to work where he was working. I mean he could be more help and so he... Robert was one of the older women and she was the one that could spank the children and get away with it. Indians don't spank their children as a rule. They're special. They're very special. They're just... Their children just mean everything to them or they did in those days. I don't know how it is now because I... And when they were noisy, especially in the church, why she would...she would scold them. And then if they didn't be good, why she would go out there and grab a hold of them and slap them. She wasn't above slapping them. She was going to have order in that church. I don't know why. And I had to laugh at a few times because I overheard some 11 year olds, 10 11 year old kids talking outside on the...this was during summer school, you know, when you have Bible school. And I was having this vacation Bible school over there and one of them said, well, you better be careful because Sophie's here today and you know, and she'll slap you since you don't behave. I'm never let on. I heard it all.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I don't know what's going to happen over there. I should go back, but my own kids didn't want me to and so I didn't. Was there a great deal of difference, real differences between the Methodist and Presbyterian congregations on the... I don't think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...the building, lap way?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I don't think so. The Indian churches have evolved. You see, they have the Catholic Church too. And some of the Indians, I didn't know any Catholics only just as I said, like Star J. Maxwell that had come in.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: He decided he didn't want to be a priest and so he wasn't, but he had taken some of the instruction.

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And Dave Miles does some of the preaching there. He works at the park now too. And Dave, we could have had Dave as a Methodist minister now, but I couldn't get those... I had gone past the time when I was doing the heavy work there. And when I came in to ask them for the $2 ,000, that's a lot of money, you know, for a bunch of church women to guarantee. And that's what he needed to go to the... because he wanted to go to school, you see, and really earn his ordination. So, the Presbyterians finally, through the Presbyterian, Indian Presbyterian Church, because they were Connectional and had a longer history, what have you, they arranged for him to go to one of their colleges. And I don't know how they arranged the scholarship, but he got the scholarship. They didn't give him any money, but they just arranged for it. And so, that's why he's Presbyterian instead of Methodist, you see. I couldn't, yeah, I couldn't do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that there is a very strong difference between Indians who are Christian and those who still follow their Indian faith, the old faith?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: There aren't too many that follow the old religion. There aren't too many now. They're either Christian members of the Christian communities. And there are several churches now. The Church of God over there has some. And the Presbyterian Church has, I don't know what their membership is. Ours was about 325 at the time I was there, which is quite a while ago. And I don't know how many are there. As I say, when I got kind of concerned about going back, like I told my Kendrick outfit, I said I'd like to go back and hear a real good witness service once again. And they couldn't imagine it. And I don't know who the younger leaders are now, because they're almost grandparents themselves, you see. And it's too bad, but that's the way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the earlier days, were you aware of discrimination against the Indians, or mixed feelings?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I understand the Indians were welcome some places and other places they weren't. There were places in Lewiston where they weren't welcome to come in to eat or anything. There was that division of opinion. Yeah. There's Indians and then there's Indians, just like there's whites and there's whites. But because they are in a white culture, and if they don't knuckle down and take on the white culture traditions, they're looked to scans. This is one of those things. I think most of them that are, for instance, the Soroptimus Club and some of us have scholarships for Lewis Clark, normal, and for the university. And two of those are supposed to be Indians. And one is in nursing because we have some wonderful Indian nurses. We have some that work where Raleigh is now. And they're dedicated and they're very fine nurses. And this is also true of whatever they do if they want to. Well, these kids go to school up here under the Johnson O 'Malley funds, you know. And they're lazy little scamps. They don't get up and get to class and they don't study and they, oh, there's a lot of things that go on up there. And somebody has to go up and prod them to do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What school is this?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: The university. And my grandson, Raleigh Stedman, named after my Raleigh, Raleigh Darryl Stedman, is at Hertworth now taking a business course. But when he got through high school, he took a year off. He'd go into his father's business, which is the glass business down there, you know. But, hey, you know. Anyway, he didn't. And finally, when he heard that there was difficulty up there and that the Johnson O 'Malley funds were about to be cut off because the kids weren't doing it and the few people that were up there that were interested, like Mrs. Lola Clyde and some of these, you know. But you couldn't expect Lola to go in there. She's my age to go in there and get those crazy, lazy kids up, you know. And because there was no body putting the thumb on them, they weren't doing it. And this came from the government and the government took their land and it kind of owes them a living, you know, this sort of thing. And Raleigh Darryl says, boy, he says, I wish I had that opportunity. He says, darn Indian kids. He says, anyhow, they don't do it. And he was swearing and say his mother took him up. She says, that's fine. I'm glad to hear you say that because your father is just willing anytime to send you to college when you make up your mind where you want to go. So dear old Raleigh Darryl had to go to college. He's at Hertworth. He didn't go to university. He went to Hertworth and is taking a special. The reason he went was because his brother, Mark Stedman, is a teacher. And he went to Hertworth and they enjoy the same one. And so that's a holdover from the Indian thing to the whites getting it back in the second, third generation. I wanted to ask you about spiritualists in Juliet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know anything about them?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I heard that the Snyder's, you know, the first settlers and there was a real movement. I don't know. I've heard so little about spiritualism. I guess it doesn't hardly exist anymore. But in those days, it did. I don't know too much about it either Sam because I know what it is. And I know that two or three people have had what I call an incident, perhaps of revelation, prophecy. You know, this ESP business. And they get that mixed up with spiritualism because, oh, there is a tradition that sometimes, you know, they say it's the Holy Ghost and God that's giving them this one. Maybe it's the devil. It's one of those things. And there is a certain, oh, pulling hole, I call it. I've made my peace with my maker and it doesn't bother me anymore. You know, it's one of these things. But after you have had a three, four, five, six hundred answers to prayer, when you really pray believing, it does something to you that doesn't leave you wishy washy. You know where this has come from. And as I said too many times, I have been caught, like I didn't know what to say. And I'd say, well, Lord, you better tell me, you know, and he's never failed me. And I'm not saying either way or the other. What I had heard was that this group in Juliet, and this is a long time ago as it dates back, that there was an idea that by digging, that there would be valuable coal found around the town. This is an historical kind of thing that I'm interested in. I don't know too much about it. I heard it too, but I'm surprised if it's being revived. Well, I don't think it's being revived now, but it's just a fact about some of the real early days around there. The, well,

speaker: Lora Albright
words: the Mormons came in, you know, and started meetings there on Sunday, because the Seventh -day Adventists, the Seventh -day Baptists and all these took some of our people from the Methodists. And they had quite a little bit to say about it. And I said, well, Juliet is an old, old town, and they have seen a great deal of change. When the Fosters, you know who Dr. Foster is, when they were there, he had a great deal to say. I thought this is what you were going to talk about, about the healing spirits. Yeah. And as far as I'm concerned, it's just one one ear and out the other, because I wasn't interested in it. But when the Mormons started there, they took, oh, Dr. Critchison, you know, and some of these are Mormons, and they took them out of our congregation. And I thought, well, I had to learn about Mormon, Sam, when I went to the legislature. Gosh, Albright, A -L -B -I -G -H -T. So when they called the roll, and you couldn't punch an electric button, they called the roll, and you had to stand up and vote I or an A. And you could, you could explain your vote, but it never got into the records. I mean, you could explain it on the floor. But there it was, down black and white. You could pass if you wanted to, but I decided that I was there to work, and I didn't pass. There was only one thing that I ever passed on, and that was on the, well, development of a freight business that needed a government license on the Columbia River. Well, it was so far away, and I didn't know a thing about it, and I passed then, and I explained that the reason I passed was because I had no knowledge upon which to base a vote. But A -L -B -R -I -G -H -T was right at the beginning of the A. Right. So you were the first people to... Ruth Cummins. I was only about five blocks from the State House, and that was a hard, hard winter, and I had to wade snow to get down there, be there at eight o 'clock every morning. And, gosh, I thought everybody read all of those state reports and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do they know how, what's going on in the state if they didn't read it?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And that's when I put my glasses on, because I had, was reading, and into the night, and many, many hours laying on a bed. This sort of thing. And then Howard Hekner and Harry Wall, and some more of them, those were the two last ones, and they get the legislation about education, for instance, and they'll say, oh, well, I'm waiting to see how Albright votes. When I see how she votes, then that's the way I'll vote. And so I was doing the reading for the whole, I mean, and I thought, well, to heck with them. All those men had been there long years before I had been there. Well, this was on educational kind of issues. Well, it was on education and also on welfare.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much difference, do you think, between the interests of North Idaho and those of South Idaho on issues like these?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: In a way. Now, the big mining interests down there, like all the gypsum and the phosphate and all that, was a lot like PFI up here. I mean, it was big business. And I didn't have any experience with big business. Miners all little business, and farming and education. I went down there, as I said, as an expert on the laws governing the schools of the state, because I had my background here. And yet the big phosphate miners and the PFI people and the Washington Water Power and all of these big companies, especially any of them that had lots of land, you know, with the taxes and what have you. Oh, I was so glad I knew a thing or two about tax.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If they had a lot of land, then what?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Then they would vote against anything that they thought was frills, either in education or welfare or in medical. One of the things that, in the medical business that I remember, they were making an assessment of all the state programs that had to do with medicine. And this meant a lot of the welfare particularly, because you get people on welfare, you know, and you can't let them die, and they have to pay the medias. And there were doctors that wasn't above getting some good old gouges in, you know, and there were even hospitals that would charge, if they were there till after breakfast, they would charge a whole day. I mean, little things like this. And it didn't seem exactly fair.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And yet who was a little housewife, farmhousewife out here, to go up against, say, the Shriners Hospital?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And all I could do was, which I did, would get my fax, and then instead of taking it to the floor or something, I would take it to them and I'd say, this is what, I'm on this committee and this is the fax that I've dug up and this is what I'm going to say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What have you got to say about it?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well, I kind of pulled their teeth a little bit because they didn't know what to do with that woman, you know, this sort of thing. But I had to learn that first. I'd go to the floor and then, gee, the screaming headlines in the newspapers, Mrs. Albright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you stand up? Did you find yourself standing up to large corporate interests?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, I mean, I did in several instances. One was, I'll never forget that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know if I should tell you, is that turned off?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: No, let's leave it on if it's about the legislature. It's something that's the public's business then in its own way. And besides, it's long gone. It's 30 years. It's 40 years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, one of the things was, how much should we allow the people on welfare for social spending, meaning to go down and get an ice cream cone or a glass of beer or what have you?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And a lot of these people were for cutting that right off. They didn't need that. They didn't need that. And I sat there listening to it that they were going to cut down their welfare checks to this amount if they could prove. And then they felt that they could do some research and they'd find out how much was being spent on liquor and maybe a motion picture show or what have you, things that they really didn't need. And then I realized that these people had a right, if they had a right to life at all, they had a right to be able to spend a couple of cents, what have you. And so I thought, well, I'm going to do some work on this. And so I did. And one of the things that I came up with finally was that the people that they were talking about were the people in poverty on welfare. People are in poverty or they wouldn't be there. And because they were there sometimes, it was because they'd been injured. It was because they had reverses, for some reason, they were on welfare in the poverty line. And so the going down to the beer parlor and buying a glass of beer or whatever it is and spending the time with there was like the club. It was their club. And so I felt that as far as I was concerned, I wouldn't mind kicking through the few more taxes and goodness knows we had plenty of taxes. To allow these people to have a little extra of the satisfactions in life of being with the people that they figured were friends to exchange ideas and to have a little socializing. And if the members of the legislature felt that they wanted to limit this, fine. And then I hesitated a little bit and I said, but you know, the urge to socialize and the urge for companionship is going to be so strong that they're going to starve their bodies and freeze their bodies in order to squeeze out the dollar or so for this socializing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I said, why should we cut it? Is anyone actually suffering? I mean, have we had any any tax based bankruptcies?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And of course they hadn't. And then I just quit. And so they went back into committee and they left it. They didn't take it off because you well, you know, I didn't have to tell you. It sounds like a like a humanitarian position to me. But it was also sensible. It was sensible.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: The welfare in the first place was to help these people over a rough spot and still you wanted to regulate them. And of course, this this is this is human nature, Sam. Whoever has the money wants the power to regulate. Now, you know that this will just go on and on and on. It just never will stop. And so I just got up and said my piece and then I sat down. That's all there was to it. And there was nothing was said for a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Finally, the presiding officer, of course, got up and he said, well, he says, what's your pleasure?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: You see, he couldn't initiate anything yet. It has to come from the floor. It has to be a motion or what have you. So one of the South Idaho legislators got up and says, I move we leave it as it is. Somebody hurried up and seconded it and they put it to a vote.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened on this hospital business, hospitalization? Was that ever an issue actually on the floor? The hospitalization?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: The question of medical costs and squeezing a little extra money out of the well. Yeah, no, it was left like it was, you see, because, yeah, because they decided not to not to do it. I was reading in the paper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's your data from?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Two places. I was very fortunate, Sam. I came into by appointment under Dr. Robbins, who was the governor then, and was a Republican governor that had taken over from a Democratic, you see, it swung back and forth. And because I don't know just how. Well, yes, I do, too. My Republican women's group called me one day and said, Laura, we would like you to be our Republican delegate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you accept?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: And I said, oh, gosh, kids, I don't know. I said, this is tough because I was working in the field and I was busy and so on and so forth. And I didn't know how much time it might take. And so I said, well, let me just take a like I'm doing now. I'll help in the daytime. I'm precinct committeeman. I can't drive after dark, so I won't be able to go to any meetings unless somebody comes by and picks me up. But I can work in the daytime and I will be distributing literature and I will be interviewing this one and that one, the other one. And I went up and met the little candidate and so on. Well, so because of this, I could call anybody in my and I kept it to my county that I knew had some of the answers. And I'd say, hey, I sure need help. And Russ Randall and some of these very fine attorneys that we had down there, I'll always be so grateful to them because he spent three hours one day talking over a bill for welfare.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Boise?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: He was down there on some other things and I needed this. Yeah, he's from Lewiston. And I never asked anybody for help that I didn't get it. And when they were talking about homesteads now, they were going to limit the people who could file on a homestead. Well, that was sheer crazy, except the rich ones that were getting poorer ones to file on those homesteads for a certain $500 or whatever. And then they would take over that whatever was left. And this happened to be down in the south. And I wasn't too, we weren't doing this up there, but it came out that I had proved up on a homestead and a lot of those people didn't know what the procedures was, you see. And so I got to tell them about that and how they did it and so on and so forth. And I said, if this goes course into law, they've got to live on that and they've got to work it and they've got to do a lot of things. This would have prevented poor people from doing it, more or less. Well, there have been strong tendencies in that direction because of the desire to have that desert land and irrigate it takes a lot of money. And not only that, but there are rich, rich minerals there and if they can get it on a homestead. You see, there's a mining act that you can lay a minor claim.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is that called?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: A mineral claim, a mineral claim, you see. But it has to be proven first that it's there. Well, you don't spend a lot of money to prove it and then have somebody else grab it up, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And if you don't have ownership to it, well, why should you?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: I mean, it's around robin, I know. Well, you were saying that you stayed up late at night and didn't have good light. You really worked hard down there. Yeah, I worked hard on it because I wanted to. I was appointed, I was a woman, and then besides, I wanted to. There were six of us. And the other Republican was our very famous Edith Miller -Cline and she was a freshman that year and she was an attorney. She always, after we worked together, she says, Laura, she said, if you had my experience and I had your gift of gab, nobody could stop us. And you see, Edith Miller -Cline has been state senator for a long time down there. When you say there were six of us, you mean six women. Yeah, there were six women.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Steen, what was her first name?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Steen was the ranking Democrat that I sure appreciated her. She was real nice to me. She was a Democrat, but we had a lot in common.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you spend time with her? Did she help you?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Yeah, we spent time together because on all of those committees, Sam, they usually put so many Republicans and so many Democrats, you see. And on women's things particularly, the six of us landed on every women's. And then after I was down there, Sam, I served on state commissions under three governors, Governor Robbins, Governor Jordan, and Governor Smiley. They appointed me and I accepted it. Raleigh was willing that I should do it. And it gave me a chance to get away from the, because we've never had money to throw away. You know, I couldn't just say, well, I'm going to Boise. But when I would take these and I felt that I must give, earn my money because they let me fly. I went a couple times on the bus, but I usually flew down and stayed at a hotel. And all I had to do was to hand in my expenses and they were paid. They were reimbursed, you see. But when you were in the legislature and you didn't have good light to work with, why did you really, because... I was poor. The poor light that I had to work with was where I stayed, you see, at Ruth Commons. And I could have had bigger bulbs than that, but I didn't think about it. See, we didn't have electricity at the time. I didn't know. And she had just one little bulb over my bed. And so that's why I was laying on the bed, you see. And if I'd had any sense at all, Sam, I'd have got a big bulb and had more light.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it hurt your eyes?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Well, it might have hurt my eyes, but well, either that or the six or eight hours a day that I was reading and really studying because I was trying to absorb a lifetime's education in about, well, while I was on the floor, really. And I was down there two sessions because they had a call session in 50 and then I was defeated in 51. And I missed by, because Lewis is a Democratic stronghold down here. And so I didn't get elected back. And the women were so upset about it. They just thought that Laura would walk right through it just because I had made a good impression down there. But you see, when it came to counting the votes, well, that was something else again. But then that's when they started appointing me to these state commissions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the commissions?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: Oh, help. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are some of them?

speaker: Lora Albright
words: The Board of Help. I was going to show you. A formula for inspection for, for all restaurants that weren't complying with the food and health people, you know, and school lunch places. And, oh, I was on the State Institutions Committee, and that's everything from the penitentiary to the bug house up here. And I had some real interesting experiences. The penitentiary really grips you when you get down there. And you know, the women of the state has never been housed in Idaho. You knew that. They've never had a women's prison.
